revision.comment: Auto generating page. date placed far in the past so it is attributed to Immanuelle and FireflyBot sends them messages if it is ever imported to Wikipedia.
revision.text: #redirect[[en:Takatoo Yoritsugu]] [[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects]]

'''高遠頼継'''('''たかとおよりつぐ''')は、地元の名門・諏訪一族の[[戦国武将]]。[[信濃国]]の[[豪族]]。
[[1542年]](天正11年)、諏訪家当主の[[諏訪頼重]]が[[武田信玄]]の謀略によって自刃すると、同年9月10日、諏訪家当主となるべく[[高遠町]]で挙兵。9月25日に[[安国寺合戦]]で敗れると、[[高遠]]の地へ逃れた。 [[1545年]](天正13年)には、武田家との講和が成立すると、高遠城を無血開城し、その後、[[甲斐国]]で隠居生活を送った。

'''高遠頼継'''('''たかとおよりつぐ''')は、地元の名門・諏訪一族の[[戦国武将]]。[[信濃国]]の[[豪族]]。
[[1542年]](天正11年)、諏訪家当主の[[諏訪頼重]]が[[武田信玄]]の謀略によって自刃すると、同年9月10日、諏訪家当主となるべく[[高遠町]]で挙兵。9月25日に[[安国寺合戦]]で敗れると、[[高遠]]の地へ逃れた。 [[1545年]](天正13年)には、武田家との講和が成立すると、[[高遠城]]を無血開城し、その後、[[甲斐国]]で隠居生活を送った。

'''高遠頼継'''('''たかとおよりつぐ'''、？ - [[天文]]21年[[1月27日 (旧暦)|1月27日]]（[[1552年]][[2月21日]]）)は、地元の名門・諏訪一族の[[戦国武将]]。[[信濃国]]の[[豪族]]。
[[1542年]](天文11年)、諏訪家当主の[[諏訪頼重]]が[[武田信玄]]の謀略によって自刃すると、同年9月10日、諏訪家当主となるべく[[高遠町]]で挙兵。9月25日に[[安国寺合戦]]で敗れると、[[高遠]]の地へ逃れた。 [[1545年]](天文13年)には、武田家との講和が成立すると、[[高遠城]]を無血開城し、その後、[[甲斐国]]で隠居生活を送った。
1552年、[[武田氏]]により自刃させられ、[[高遠氏]]は滅亡した。

[[1542年]]（天文11年）、諏訪家当主の[[諏訪頼重 (刑部大輔)|諏訪頼重]]が[[武田信玄]]の謀略によって自刃すると、同年9月10日、諏訪家当主となるべく高遠の地で挙兵。9月25日に[[安国寺合戦]]で敗れると、高遠の地へ逃れた。 [[1545年]]（天文13年）に、武田家との講和が成立すると、[[高遠城]]を無血開城した。その後は[[甲斐国]]で隠居生活を送る。

{{出典の明記}} '''高遠 頼継'''（たかとお よりつぐ、？ - [[天文_(元号)|天文]]21年[[1月27日 (旧暦)|1月27日]]（[[1552年]][[2月21日]]））は、[[戦国時代 (日本)|戦国時代]]の武将で、[[信濃国|信濃]]の名門・[[諏訪氏]]庶流の[[高遠氏]]当主。受領名は紀伊守。
[[1542年]]（天文11年）、諏訪家当主の[[諏訪頼重 (刑部大輔)|諏訪頼重]]が[[武田信玄]]の謀略によって自刃すると、同年9月10日、諏訪家当主となるべく高遠の地で挙兵。9月25日に[[安国寺合戦]]で敗れると、高遠の地へ逃れた。 [[1545年]]（天文13年）に、武田家との講和が成立すると、[[高遠城]]を無血開城した。その後は[[甲斐国]]で隠居生活を送る。
1552年、[[武田氏]]により自刃させられ、[[高遠氏]]は滅亡した。
==関連項目== ==参考文献== ==外部リンク== [[category:諏訪氏|たかとおよりつく]] [[category:戦国武将|たかとおよりつく]] [[Category:1552年没|たかとおよりつく]]

{{出典の明記}} '''高遠 頼継'''（たかとお よりつぐ、？ - [[天文_(元号)|天文]]21年[[1月27日 (旧暦)|1月27日]]（[[1552年]][[2月21日]]））は、[[戦国時代 (日本)|戦国時代]]の武将で、[[信濃国|信濃]]の名門・[[諏訪氏]]庶流の[[高遠氏]]当主。受領名は紀伊守。
諏訪氏の文明の内訌の際に惣領家と対立した[[高遠継宗]]の子とも孫（父は[[高遠満継]]）ともいわれる。[[諏訪頼満]]が諏訪氏を統一すると抵抗するもその傘下となった。のちに頼満の娘を妻に迎えている。
[[1542年]]（天文11年）、諏訪家当主の[[諏訪頼重 (刑部大輔)|諏訪頼重]]が[[武田信玄]]の謀略によって自刃すると、同年9月10日、諏訪家当主となるべく高遠の地で挙兵。9月25日に[[安国寺合戦]]で敗れると、高遠の地へ逃れた。 [[1545年]]（天文13年）に、武田家との講和が成立すると、[[高遠城]]を無血開城した。その後は[[甲斐国]]で隠居生活を送る。[[上田原の戦い]]で[[板垣信方]]が戦死し、諏訪地方が混乱すると、一時的に復帰し、武田氏のために[[藤沢頼継]]と対峙したものの、その力をかえって武田氏に警戒されることになり、1552年、自刃させられ、[[高遠氏]]は滅亡した。

{{出典の明記}} '''高遠 頼継'''（たかとお よりつぐ、？ - [[天文_(元号)|天文]]21年[[1月27日 (旧暦)|1月27日]]（[[1552年]][[2月21日]]））は、[[戦国時代 (日本)|戦国時代]]の武将で、[[信濃国|信濃]]の名門・[[諏訪氏]]庶流の[[高遠氏]]当主。受領名は紀伊守。
諏訪氏の文明の内訌の際に惣領家と対立した[[高遠継宗]]の子とも孫（父は[[高遠満継]]）ともいわれる。[[諏訪頼満]]が諏訪氏を統一すると抵抗するもその傘下となった。のちに頼満の娘を妻に迎えている。
[[1542年]]（天文11年）、諏訪家当主の[[諏訪頼重 (刑部大輔)|諏訪頼重]]が[[武田信玄]]の謀略によって自刃すると、同年9月10日、諏訪家当主となるべく高遠の地で挙兵。9月25日に[[安国寺合戦]]で敗れると、高遠の地へ逃れた。 [[1545年]]（天文13年）に、武田家との講和が成立すると、[[高遠城]]を無血開城した。その後は[[甲斐国]]で隠居生活を送る。[[上田原の戦い]]で[[板垣信方]]が戦死し、諏訪地方が混乱すると、一時的に復帰し、武田氏のために[[藤沢頼親]]と対峙したものの、その力をかえって武田氏に警戒されることになり、1552年、自刃させられ、[[高遠氏]]は滅亡した。

[[1542年]]（天文11年）、諏訪家当主の[[諏訪頼重 (刑部大輔)|諏訪頼重]]が[[武田晴信]]の謀略によって自刃すると、同年9月10日、諏訪家当主となるべく高遠の地で挙兵。9月25日に[[安国寺合戦]]で敗れると、高遠の地へ逃れた。 [[1545年]]（天文13年）に、武田家との講和が成立すると、[[高遠城]]を無血開城した。その後は[[甲斐国]]で隠居生活を送る。[[上田原の戦い]]で[[板垣信方]]が戦死し、諏訪地方が混乱すると、一時的に復帰し、武田氏のために[[藤沢頼親]]と対峙したものの、その力をかえって武田氏に警戒されることになり、1552年、自刃させられ、[[高遠氏]]は滅亡した。

[[1542年]]（天文11年）、諏訪家当主の[[諏訪頼重 (刑部大輔)|諏訪頼重]]が[[武田晴信]]の謀略によって自刃すると、同年9月10日、諏訪家当主となるべく高遠の地で挙兵。9月25日に[[安国寺合戦]]で敗れると、高遠の地へ逃れた。 [[1545年]]（天文13年）に、武田家との講和が成立すると、[[高遠城]]を無血開城した。その後は[[甲斐国]]で隠居生活を送る。[[上田原の戦い]]で[[板垣信方]]が戦死し、諏訪地方が混乱すると、一時的に復帰し、武田氏のために[[藤沢頼親]]と対峙したものの、その力をかえって武田氏に警戒されることになり、1552年、自刃させられ、[[高遠氏]]は滅亡した。

'''高遠 頼継'''（たかとお よりつぐ、？ - [[天文_(元号)|天文]]21年[[8月16日 (旧暦)|8月16日]]（[[1552年]]）は、[[戦国時代 (日本)|戦国時代]]の武将で、[[信濃国|信濃]]の名門・[[諏訪氏]]庶流の[[高遠氏]]当主。受領名は紀伊守。
諏訪氏の文明の内訌の際に惣領家と対立した[[高遠継宗]]の子とも孫（父は[[高遠満継]]）ともいわれる。[[諏訪頼満]]が諏訪氏を統一すると抵抗するもその傘下となった。のちに頼満の娘を妻に迎えている。
[[1542年]]（天文11年）、諏訪家当主の[[諏訪頼重 (刑部大輔)|諏訪頼重]]が[[武田信玄|武田晴信]]の謀略によって自刃すると、同年9月10日、諏訪家当主となるべく高遠の地で挙兵。9月25日に[[安国寺合戦]]で敗れると、高遠の地へ逃れた。 [[1545年]]（天文13年）に、武田家との講和が成立すると、[[高遠城]]を無血開城した。その後は[[甲斐国]]で隠居生活を送る。[[上田原の戦い]]で[[板垣信方]]が戦死し、諏訪地方が混乱すると、一時的に復帰し、武田氏のために[[藤沢頼親]]と対峙したものの、その力をかえって武田氏に警戒されることになり、1552年、自刃させられ、[[高遠氏]]は滅亡した。

'''高遠 頼継'''（たかとお よりつぐ、？ - [[天文_(元号)|天文]]21年[[8月16日 (旧暦)|8月16日]]（[[1552年]]）は、[[戦国時代 (日本)|戦国時代]]の武将で、[[信濃国|信濃]]の名門・[[諏訪氏]]庶流の[[高遠氏]]当主。受領名は紀伊守。
諏訪氏の文明の内訌の際に惣領家と対立した[[高遠継宗]]の子とも孫（父は[[高遠満継]]）ともいわれる。[[諏訪頼満]]が諏訪氏を統一すると抵抗するもその傘下となった。のちに頼満の娘を妻に迎えている。
[[1542年]]（天文11年）、諏訪家当主の[[諏訪頼重 (戦国時代)|諏訪頼重]]が[[武田信玄|武田晴信]]の謀略によって自刃すると、同年9月10日、諏訪家当主となるべく高遠の地で挙兵。9月25日に[[安国寺合戦]]で敗れると、高遠の地へ逃れた。 [[1545年]]（天文13年）に、武田家との講和が成立すると、[[高遠城]]を無血開城した。その後は[[甲斐国]]で隠居生活を送る。[[上田原の戦い]]で[[板垣信方]]が戦死し、諏訪地方が混乱すると、一時的に復帰し、武田氏のために[[藤沢頼親]]と対峙したものの、その力をかえって武田氏に警戒されることになり、1552年、自刃させられ、[[高遠氏]]は滅亡した。
==関連項目== * [[高遠継宗]] * [[高遠頼宗|高遠連峰軒]]（頼宗） <!-- ==参考文献== ==外部リンク== --> {{DEFAULTSORT:たかとお よりつく}} [[category:戦国武将]] [[category:高遠氏|よりつく]] [[Category:1552年没]]

'''高遠 頼継'''（たかとお よりつぐ、？ - [[天文_(元号)|天文]]21年[[8月16日 (旧暦)|8月16日]]（[[1552年]]？）は、[[戦国時代 (日本)|戦国時代]]の武将。[[信濃国]]の国衆で、[[伊那郡]][[高遠城]]（[[長野県]][[伊那市]][[高遠町]]）城主。甲斐武田氏の家臣で信濃先方衆。諏訪郡の領主[[諏訪氏]]庶流の[[高遠氏]]の当主で、諏訪姓も称している。受領名は信濃守、紀伊守。父は満継（信濃守）で嫡男。正室は諏訪頼満の娘。
== 略歴 == 諏訪氏の文明の内訌の際に惣領家と対立した[[高遠継宗]]の子とも孫（父は[[高遠満継]]）ともいわれる。[[諏訪頼満]]が諏訪氏を統一すると抵抗するもその傘下となった。のちに頼満の娘を妻に迎えている。
戦国期に甲斐国守護武田氏と信濃[[諏訪郡]]の諏訪氏は同盟関係を結んでいたが、天文10年（1542年）6月に武田氏では[[武田信玄|武田晴信]]（信玄）が当主になると、晴信は[[信濃侵攻]]を本格化させ、諏訪氏との同盟を破棄して諏訪郡へ侵攻する。翌天文11年7月2日、頼継は武田氏の諏訪侵攻に与し、[[諏訪頼重 (戦国時代)|諏訪頼重]]の本拠[[上原城]]（長野県[[諏訪市]]）へ侵攻する。頼重は武田方に降伏すると同年7月に[[甲府]]へ護送され自刃する（『[[高白斎記]]』『[[守矢頼真書留]]』）。諏訪領は武田氏と分割され頼継は西半分を支配していたが、諏訪氏惣領を志向する頼継は[[伊那郡]][[福与城]]の[[藤沢頼親]]らと武田領へ侵攻し、同年9月25日には諏訪宮川端において武田方に敗退し、諏訪から退去した（『高白斎記』）。
武田方はさらに伊那の藤沢頼親や小県郡長窪城の大井貞隆らを攻め、天文14年（[[1545年]]）4月17日には高遠城も落城し、頼継も武田方に降伏し、甲府へ出仕する（『高白斎記』）。高遠城はその後武田氏により改修され、信濃支配における拠点となる。天文17年（1548年）2月14日、武田方は[[小県郡]]の[[村上義清]]との[[上田原の戦い]]において敗退すると同年7月に諏訪西方衆が謀反を起こすなど武田の支配領域では動揺が起こるが（『高白斎記』『勝山記』）、頼継は同年4月3日に甲府から高遠城へ帰城している（『高白斎記』）。その後再び武田方に出仕しているが、天文21年（1552年）8月16日には死去（自害か）しており、[[高遠氏]]は滅亡した。法名は大用普徹す禅定院。
==関連項目== * [[高遠継宗]] * [[高遠頼宗|高遠連峰軒]]（頼宗）
==参考文献== * [[黒田基樹]]「高遠頼継」『武田信玄大辞典』
{{DEFAULTSORT:たかとお よりつく}} [[category:戦国武将]] [[category:高遠氏|よりつく]] [[Category:1552年没]]

'''高遠 頼継'''（たかとお よりつぐ、生年未詳 - [[天文_(元号)|天文]]21年[[8月16日 (旧暦)|8月16日]]（[[1552年]]？）は、[[戦国時代 (日本)|戦国時代]]の武将。[[信濃国]]の国衆で、[[伊那郡]][[高遠城]]（[[長野県]][[伊那市]][[高遠町]]）城主。甲斐武田氏の家臣で信濃先方衆。諏訪郡の領主[[諏訪氏]]庶流の[[高遠氏]]の当主で、諏訪姓も称している。受領名は信濃守、紀伊守。父は満継（信濃守）で嫡男。正室は[[諏訪頼満 (安芸守)|諏訪頼満]]の娘。

戦国期に甲斐国守護武田氏と信濃[[諏訪郡]]の諏訪氏は同盟関係を結んでいたが、天文10年（1542年）6月に武田氏では[[武田信玄|武田晴信]]（信玄）が当主になると、晴信は[[信濃侵攻]]を本格化させ、諏訪氏との同盟を破棄して諏訪郡へ侵攻する。翌天文11年7月2日、頼継は武田氏の諏訪侵攻に与し、[[諏訪頼重 (戦国時代)|諏訪頼重]]の本拠[[上原城]]（長野県[[諏訪市]]）へ侵攻する。頼重は武田方に降伏すると同年7月に[[甲府]]へ護送され自刃する（『[[高白斎記]]』『[[守矢頼真書留]]』）。諏訪領は武田氏と分割され頼継は西半分を支配していたが、諏訪氏惣領を志向する頼継は[[伊那郡]][[福与城]]の[[藤沢頼親]]らと武田領へ侵攻し、同年9月25日には諏訪宮川端において武田方に敗退し、諏訪から退去した（『高白斎記』）。
武田方はさらに伊那の藤沢頼親や小県郡[[長窪城]]の[[大井貞隆]]らを攻め、天文14年（[[1545年]]）4月17日には高遠城も落城し、頼継も武田方に降伏し、甲府へ出仕する（『高白斎記』）。高遠城はその後武田氏により改修され、信濃支配における拠点となる。天文17年（1548年）2月14日、武田方は[[小県郡]]の[[村上義清]]との[[上田原の戦い]]において敗退すると同年7月に諏訪西方衆が謀反を起こすなど武田の支配領域では動揺が起こるが（『高白斎記』『勝山記』）、頼継は同年4月3日に甲府から高遠城へ帰城している（『高白斎記』）。その後再び武田方に出仕しているが、天文21年（1552年）8月16日には死去（自害か）しており、[[高遠氏]]は滅亡した。法名は大用普徹す禅定院。
== 関連項目 == * [[高遠継宗]] * [[高遠頼宗|高遠蓮峰軒]]（頼宗）
== 参考文献 == * [[黒田基樹]]「高遠頼継」『武田信玄大辞典』
{{DEFAULTSORT:たかとお よりつく}} [[category:戦国武将]] [[category:高遠氏|よりつく]] [[Category:1552年没]]

'''高遠 頼継'''（たかとお よりつぐ、生年未詳 - [[天文_(元号)|天文]]21年[[8月16日 (旧暦)|8月16日]]（[[1552年]][[9月4日]]）？）は、[[戦国時代 (日本)|戦国時代]]の武将。[[信濃国]]の国衆で、[[伊那郡]][[高遠城]]（[[長野県]][[伊那市]][[高遠町]]）城主。甲斐武田氏の家臣で信濃先方衆。諏訪郡の領主[[諏訪氏]]庶流の[[高遠氏]]の当主で、諏訪姓も称している。受領名は信濃守、紀伊守。父は満継（信濃守）で嫡男。正室は[[諏訪頼満 (安芸守)|諏訪頼満]]の娘。
== 略歴 == 諏訪氏の文明の内訌の際に惣領家と対立した[[高遠継宗]]の子とも孫（父は[[高遠満継]]）ともいわれる。[[諏訪頼満 (安芸守)|諏訪頼満]]が諏訪氏を統一すると抵抗するもその傘下となった。のちに頼満の娘を妻に迎えている。
戦国期に甲斐国守護武田氏と信濃[[諏訪郡]]の諏訪氏は同盟関係を結んでいたが、天文10年（1542年）6月に武田氏では[[武田信玄|武田晴信]]（信玄）が当主になると、晴信は[[信濃侵攻]]を本格化させ、諏訪氏との同盟を破棄して諏訪郡へ侵攻する。翌天文11年7月2日、頼継は武田氏の諏訪侵攻に与し、[[諏訪頼重 (戦国時代)|諏訪頼重]]の本拠[[上原城]]（長野県[[諏訪市]]）へ侵攻する。頼重は武田方に降伏すると同年7月に[[甲府]]へ護送され自刃する（『[[高白斎記]]』『[[守矢頼真書留]]』）。諏訪領は武田氏と分割され頼継は西半分を支配していたが、諏訪氏惣領を志向する頼継は[[伊那郡]][[福与城]]の[[藤沢頼親]]らと武田領へ侵攻し、同年9月25日には諏訪宮川端において武田方に敗退し、諏訪から退去した（『高白斎記』）。
武田方はさらに伊那の藤沢頼親や小県郡[[長窪城]]の[[大井貞隆]]らを攻め、天文14年（[[1545年]]）4月17日には高遠城も落城し、頼継も武田方に降伏し、甲府へ出仕する（『高白斎記』）。高遠城はその後武田氏により改修され、信濃支配における拠点となる。天文17年（1548年）2月14日、武田方は[[小県郡]]の[[村上義清]]との[[上田原の戦い]]において敗退すると同年7月に諏訪西方衆が謀反を起こすなど武田の支配領域では動揺が起こるが（『高白斎記』『勝山記』）、頼継は同年4月3日に甲府から高遠城へ帰城している（『高白斎記』）。
その後再び武田方に出仕しているが、天文21年（1552年）8月16日には死去（自害か）している。法名は大用普徹大禅定院。
[[高遠氏]]は従来頼継の死去をもって滅亡したと考えられていたが、近年は高野山成慶院に伝来する『甲斐国過去帳』の記載から、[[永禄]]5年（[[1562年]]）に諏訪惣領家を継いだと見られていた信玄四男の[[武田勝頼|勝頼]]が高遠諏訪家を継いでいたことが指摘されている。
== 関連項目 == * [[高遠継宗]] * [[高遠頼宗|高遠蓮峰軒]]（頼宗）
== 参考文献 == * [[黒田基樹]]「高遠頼継」『武田信玄大辞典』
{{DEFAULTSORT:たかとお よりつく}} [[category:戦国武将]] [[category:高遠氏|よりつく]] [[Category:1552年没]]

戦国期に甲斐国守護武田氏と信濃[[諏訪郡]]の諏訪氏は同盟関係を結んでいたが、天文10年（1542年）6月に武田氏では[[武田信玄|武田晴信]]（信玄）が当主になると、晴信は[[信濃侵攻]]を本格化させ、諏訪氏との同盟を破棄して諏訪郡へ侵攻する。翌天文11年7月2日、頼継は武田氏の諏訪侵攻に与し、[[諏訪頼重 (戦国時代)|諏訪頼重]]の本拠[[上原城]]（長野県[[諏訪市]]）へ侵攻する。頼重は武田方に降伏すると同年7月に[[甲府]]へ護送され自刃する（『[[高白斎記]]』『[[守矢頼真書留]]』）。諏訪領は武田氏と分割され頼継は西半分を支配していたが、諏訪氏惣領を志向する頼継は[[伊那郡]][[福与城]]の[[藤沢頼親]]らと武田領へ侵攻し、同年9月25日には諏訪宮川端において武田方に敗退し、諏訪から退去した（『高白斎記』）。
武田方はさらに伊那の藤沢頼親や小県郡[[長窪城]]の[[大井貞隆]]らを攻め、天文14年（[[1545年]]）4月17日には高遠城も落城し、頼継も武田方に降伏し、甲府へ出仕する（『高白斎記』）。高遠城はその後武田氏により改修され、信濃支配における拠点となる。天文17年（1548年）2月14日、武田方は[[小県郡]]の[[村上義清]]との[[上田原の戦い]]において敗退すると同年7月に諏訪西方衆が謀反を起こすなど武田の支配領域では動揺が起こるが（『高白斎記』『勝山記』）、頼継は同年4月3日に甲府から高遠城へ帰城している（『高白斎記』）。
その後再び武田方に出仕しているが、天文21年（1552年）8月16日には死去（自害か）している。法名は大用普徹大禅定院。
[[高遠氏]]は従来頼継の死去をもって滅亡したと考えられていたが、近年は高野山成慶院に伝来する『甲斐国過去帳』の記載から、[[永禄]]5年（[[1562年]]）に諏訪惣領家を継いだと見られていた信玄四男の[[武田勝頼|勝頼]]が高遠諏訪家を継いでいたことが指摘されている。
== 関連項目 == * [[高遠継宗]] * [[高遠頼宗|高遠蓮峰軒]]（頼宗）

revision.text:
'''高遠 頼継'''（たかとお よりつぐ、生年未詳 - [[天文_(元号)|天文]]21年[[8月16日 (旧暦)|8月16日]]（[[1552年]][[9月4日]]）？）は、[[戦国時代 (日本)|戦国時代]]の武将。[[信濃国]]の国衆で、[[伊那郡]][[高遠城]]（[[長野県]][[伊那市]][[高遠町]]）城主。甲斐武田氏の家臣で信濃先方衆。諏訪郡の領主[[諏訪氏]]庶流の[[高遠氏]]の当主で、諏訪姓も称している。受領名は信濃守、紀伊守。父は満継（信濃守）で嫡男。正室は[[諏訪頼満 (安芸守)|諏訪頼満]]の娘。
== 略歴 == 諏訪氏の文明の内訌の際に惣領家と対立した[[高遠継宗]]の子とも孫（父は[[高遠満継]]）ともいわれる。[[諏訪頼満 (安芸守)|諏訪頼満]]が諏訪氏を統一すると抵抗するもその傘下となった。のちに頼満の娘を妻に迎えている。
戦国期に甲斐国守護武田氏と信濃[[諏訪郡]]の諏訪氏は同盟関係を結んでいたが、天文10年（1542年）6月に武田氏では[[武田信玄|武田晴信]]（信玄）が当主になると、晴信は[[信濃侵攻]]を本格化させ、諏訪氏との同盟を破棄して諏訪郡へ侵攻する。翌天文11年7月2日、頼継は武田氏の諏訪侵攻に与し、[[諏訪頼重 (戦国時代)|諏訪頼重]]の本拠[[上原城]]（長野県[[諏訪市]]）へ侵攻する。頼重は武田方に降伏すると同年7月に[[甲府]]へ護送され自刃する（『[[高白斎記]]』『[[守矢頼真書留]]』）。諏訪領は宮川を境に武田氏と分割され頼継は西半分を支配していたが、諏訪氏惣領を志向する頼継は[[伊那郡]][[福与城]]の[[藤沢頼親]]らと武田領へ侵攻する。しかし同年9月25日には[[宮川の戦い]]において武田方に敗退し、諏訪から退去した（『高白斎記』）。
武田方はさらに伊那の藤沢頼親や小県郡[[長窪城]]の[[大井貞隆]]らを攻め、天文14年（[[1545年]]）4月17日には高遠城も落城し（[[高遠合戦]]）、頼継も武田方に降伏し、甲府へ出仕する（『高白斎記』）。高遠城はその後武田氏により改修され、信濃支配における拠点となる。天文17年（1548年）2月14日、武田方は[[小県郡]]の[[村上義清]]との[[上田原の戦い]]において敗退すると同年7月に諏訪西方衆が謀反を起こすなど武田の支配領域では動揺が起こるが（『高白斎記』『勝山記』）、頼継は同年4月3日に甲府から高遠城へ帰城している（『高白斎記』）。
その後、再び武田氏に出仕しているが、天文21年（1552年）の下伊那攻めの際に自害させらた。法名は大用普徹大禅定院。それに代わって[[高遠氏]]重臣の[[保科正俊]]が武田氏に重用されるようになった。高遠氏は従来、頼継の死去をもって滅亡したと考えられていたが、近年は[[高野山]]成慶院に伝来する『甲斐国過去帳』の記載から、[[永禄]]5年（[[1562年]]）に諏訪惣領家を継いだと見られていた信玄四男の[[武田勝頼|勝頼]]が高遠諏訪家を継いでいたことが指摘されている。
== 関連項目 == * [[高遠継宗]] * [[高遠頼宗|高遠蓮峰軒]]（頼宗）
== 参考文献 == * [[黒田基樹]]「高遠頼継」『武田信玄大辞典』 * 田中豊茂「信濃中世武家伝」[[信濃毎日新聞|信濃毎日新聞社]] 2016年
{{DEFAULTSORT:たかとお よりつく}} [[category:戦国武将]] [[category:高遠氏|よりつく]] [[Category:信濃国の人物]] [[Category:生年不明]] [[Category:1552年没]]

== 略歴 == 諏方氏の文明の内訌の際に惣領家と対立した[[高遠継宗]]の子とも孫（父は[[高遠満継]]）ともいわれる。[[諏訪頼満 (安芸守)|諏方頼満]]が諏方氏を統一すると抵抗するもその傘下となった。のちに頼満の娘を妻に迎えている。

武田方はさらに伊那の[[藤沢頼親]]や小県郡[[長窪城]]の[[大井貞隆]]らを攻め、天文14年（[[1545年]]）4月17日には高遠城も落城し（[[高遠合戦]]）、頼継も武田方に降伏し、甲府へ出仕する（『高白斎記』）。高遠城はその後武田氏により改修され、信濃支配における拠点となる。天文17年（1548年）2月14日、武田方は[[小県郡]]の[[村上義清]]との[[上田原の戦い]]において敗退すると同年7月に諏方西方衆が謀反を起こすなど武田の支配領域では動揺が起こるが（『高白斎記』『勝山記』）、頼継は同年4月3日に甲府から高遠城へ帰城している（『高白斎記』）。
その後、再び武田氏に出仕しているが、天文21年（1552年）の下伊那攻めの際に自害させられた。法名は大用普徹大禅定院。それに代わって[[高遠氏]]重臣の[[保科正俊]]が武田氏に重用されるようになった。高遠氏は従来、頼継の死去をもって滅亡したと考えられていたが、近年は[[高野山]]成慶院に伝来する『甲斐国過去帳』の記載から、[[永禄]]5年（[[1562年]]）に諏方惣領家を継いだと見られていた信玄四男の[[武田勝頼|勝頼]]が高遠諏方家を継いでいたことが指摘されている。
== 関連項目 == * [[高遠頼宗|高遠蓮峰軒（頼宗）]] *[[武田勝頼|諏方（武田）勝頼]]
== 参考文献 == * [[黒田基樹]]「高遠頼継」『武田信玄大辞典』 * 田中豊茂「信濃中世武家伝」[[信濃毎日新聞|信濃毎日新聞社]] 2016年
{{DEFAULTSORT:たかとお よりつく}} [[category:戦国武将]] [[Category:信濃国人領主]] [[category:高遠氏|よりつく]] [[Category:生年不明]] [[Category:1552年没]]

== 参考文献 == * [[黒田基樹]]「高遠頼継」『武田信玄大辞典』 * 田中豊茂「信濃中世武家伝」[[信濃毎日新聞|信濃毎日新聞社]] 2016年 *[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%AB%8F%E8%A8%AA%E9%A0%BC%E7%B6%99-85402 コトバンク・諏訪頼継] {{DEFAULTSORT:たかとお よりつく}} [[category:戦国武将]] [[Category:信濃国人領主]] [[category:高遠氏|よりつく]] [[Category:生年不明]] [[Category:1552年没]]

'''高遠 頼継'''（たかとお よりつぐ、生年未詳 - [[天文_(元号)|天文]]21年[[8月16日 (旧暦)|8月16日]]（[[1552年]][[9月4日]]）?）は、[[戦国時代 (日本)|戦国時代]]の[[武将]]。[[信濃国]]の国衆で、[[伊那郡]][[高遠城]]（[[長野県]][[伊那市]][[高遠町]]）城主。甲斐武田氏の家臣で信濃先方衆。諏訪郡の領主[[諏訪氏]]庶流の[[高遠氏]]の当主で、諏訪姓も称している。受領名は信濃守、紀伊守。父は満継（信濃守）で嫡男。正室は[[諏訪頼満 (安芸守)|諏訪頼満]]の娘。
== 略歴 == 諏訪氏の文明の内訌の際に惣領家と対立した[[高遠継宗]]の子とも孫（父は[[高遠満継]]）ともいわれる。[[諏訪頼満 (安芸守)|諏訪頼満]]が諏訪氏を統一すると抵抗するもその傘下となった。のちに頼満の娘を妻に迎えている。
戦国期に甲斐国守護武田氏と信濃[[諏訪郡]]の諏訪氏は同盟関係を結んでいたが、天文10年（1542年）6月に武田氏では[[武田信玄|武田晴信]]（信玄）が当主になると、晴信は[[信濃侵攻]]を本格化させ、諏訪氏との同盟を破棄して諏訪郡へ侵攻する。翌天文11年7月2日、頼継は武田氏の諏訪侵攻に与し、[[諏訪頼重 (戦国時代)|諏訪頼重]]の本拠[[上原城]]（長野県[[諏訪市]]）へ侵攻する。頼重は武田方に降伏すると同年7月に[[甲府]]へ護送され自刃する（『[[高白斎記]]』『[[守矢頼真書留]]』）。諏訪領は宮川を境に武田氏と分割され頼継は西半分を支配していたが、諏訪氏惣領を志向する頼継は[[伊那郡]][[福与城]]の[[藤沢頼親]]らと武田領へ侵攻する。しかし同年9月25日には[[宮川の戦い]]において武田方に敗退し、諏訪から退去した（『高白斎記』）。
武田方はさらに伊那の[[藤沢頼親]]や小県郡[[長窪城]]の[[大井貞隆]]らを攻め、天文14年（[[1545年]]）4月17日には高遠城も落城し（[[高遠合戦]]）、頼継も武田方に降伏し、甲府へ出仕する（『高白斎記』）。高遠城はその後武田氏により改修され、信濃支配における拠点となる。天文17年（1548年）2月14日、武田方は[[小県郡]]の[[村上義清]]との[[上田原の戦い]]において敗退すると同年7月に諏訪西方衆が謀反を起こすなど武田の支配領域では動揺が起こるが（『高白斎記』『勝山記』）、頼継は同年4月3日に甲府から高遠城へ帰城している（『高白斎記』）。
その後、再び武田氏に出仕しているが、天文21年（1552年）の下伊那攻めの際に自害させられた。法名は大用普徹大禅定院。それに代わって[[高遠氏]]重臣の[[保科正俊]]が武田氏に重用されるようになった。高遠氏は従来、頼継の死去をもって滅亡したと考えられていたが、近年は[[高野山]]成慶院に伝来する『甲斐国過去帳』の記載から、[[永禄]]5年（[[1562年]]）に諏訪惣領家を継いだと見られていた信玄四男の[[武田勝頼|勝頼]]が高遠諏訪家を継いでいたことが指摘されている<ref>丸島和洋「高野山成慶院『甲斐国供養帳』－『過去帳（甲州月牌帳）』－」『武田氏研究』34号、2006年。</ref>。
== 関連項目 == * [[高遠継宗]] * [[高遠頼宗|高遠蓮峰軒]]（頼宗）
== 参考文献 == * [[黒田基樹]]「高遠頼継」『武田信玄大辞典』 * 田中豊茂「信濃中世武家伝」[[信濃毎日新聞|信濃毎日新聞社]] 2016年 *[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%AB%8F%E8%A8%AA%E9%A0%BC%E7%B6%99-85402 コトバンク・諏訪頼継] {{DEFAULTSORT:たかとお よりつく}} [[category:戦国武将]] [[Category:信濃国人領主]] [[category:高遠氏|よりつく]] [[Category:生年不明]] [[Category:1552年没]]

'''Takato Yoritsugu''' (高遠 頼継, birth year unknown – [[Tenbun]] 21, [[August 16 (lunar calendar)|August 16]] ([[September 4]], [[1552]])?) was a [[{{ill|Samurai|ja|武士}}]] during Japan’s [[{{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代 (日本)}}]]. He was a local lord ([[kokujin]]) of [[{{ill|Shinano Province|ja|信濃国}}]], serving as the lord of [[{{ill|Takato Castle|ja|高遠城}}]] (located in present-day [[{{ill|Ina, Nagano|ja|伊那市}}]], [[{{ill|Takato Town|ja|高遠町}}]]). As a retainer of the [[{{ill|Takeda clan|ja|甲斐武田氏}}]], he held the position of [[{{ill|Sakikata-shū|ja|信濃先方衆}}]] in charge of the Shinano frontlines. He belonged to the [[{{ill|Takato clan|ja|高遠氏}}]], a cadet branch of the [[{{ill|Suwa clan|ja|諏訪氏}}]], and sometimes used the Suwa surname. His official titles included Shinano-no-Kami and Kii-no-Kami. Yoritsugu was the son and heir of Takato Mitsunari (Shinano-no-Kami) and married the daughter of [[{{ill|Suwa Yorimitsu (Aki-no-Kami)|ja|諏訪頼満 (安芸守)}}]].
== Biography == Yoritsugu is said to have been the son or grandson of [[{{ill|Takato Tsugimune|ja|高遠継宗}}]], who opposed the Suwa main family during internal conflicts. After [[{{ill|Suwa Yorimitsu (Aki-no-Kami)|ja|諏訪頼満}}]] unified the Suwa clan, Yoritsugu initially resisted but eventually became a vassal and married Yorimitsu’s daughter.
During the Sengoku period, the Takeda clan of [[{{ill|Kai Province|ja|甲斐国}}]] and the Suwa clan of Shinano Province had a long-standing alliance. However, in Tenbun 10 (1542), [[{{ill|Takeda Harunobu (Shingen)|ja|武田晴信}}]] (later known as Takeda Shingen) ascended as the Takeda clan head and began an aggressive campaign to conquer Shinano, breaking the alliance and invading the Suwa domain. On [[July 2]], Tenbun 11 (1542), Yoritsugu assisted the Takeda in attacking [[{{ill|Uehara Castle|ja|上原城}}]], the base of [[{{ill|Suwa Yorishige|ja|諏訪頼重}}]], who surrendered to the Takeda. Yorishige was taken to [[{{ill|Kōfu|ja|甲府}}]] and forced to commit suicide later that month. The Suwa domain was divided between the Takeda and Yoritsugu, with Yoritsugu controlling the western half.
Yoritsugu, aiming to restore the Suwa clan's authority, allied with [[{{ill|Fujisawa Yorichika|ja|藤沢頼親}}]] of [[{{ill|Fukuyo Castle|ja|福与城}}]] in [[{{ill|Ina District, Nagano|ja|伊那郡}}]] to invade Takeda territory. However, on [[September 25]], he was defeated in the [[{{ill|Battle of Miyagawa (1542)|ja|宮川の戦い}}]] and forced to retreat from the Suwa region.
In Tenbun 14 (1545), Takeda forces attacked the allied Fujisawa Yorichika and [[{{ill|Ōi Sadataka|ja|大井貞隆}}]] of [[{{ill|Nagakubo Castle|ja|長窪城}}]]. Takato Castle fell to the Takeda on [[April 17]], and Yoritsugu surrendered, traveling to Kōfu to submit to Takeda authority. After the castle’s capture, the Takeda fortified Takato Castle as a strategic base for controlling Shinano.
In Tenbun 17 (1548), after the Takeda’s defeat at the [[{{ill|Battle of Uedahara|ja|上田原の戦い}}]] against [[{{ill|Murakami Yoshikiyo|ja|村上義清}}]], unrest spread in Takeda-controlled territories. Yoritsugu returned to Takato Castle on [[April 3]] (as noted in the *Kōhakusai-ki*).
Yoritsugu continued serving the Takeda but was reportedly forced to commit suicide during a campaign in [[{{ill|Shimoinadani|ja|下伊那}}]] in Tenbun 21 (1552). His posthumous name was 大用普徹大禅定院 (Daiyō Futtetsu Daizendō-in). Following his death, [[{{ill|Hoshina Masatoshi|ja|保科正俊}}]], a vassal of the Takato clan, rose to prominence under the Takeda. While traditionally believed to mark the Takato clan's extinction, recent studies suggest that [[{{ill|Takeda Katsuyori|ja|武田勝頼}}]], Takeda Shingen's fourth son, inherited the Takato-Suwa lineage.<ref>Marushima Kazuhiro, "Kōyasan Jōkei-in's *Kōkoku Kako-chō*: The *Past Records of Kōshū Monthly Memorials*," *Takeda Clan Studies,* No. 34, 2006.</ref>
== References == {{reflist}}
== Sources == * [[Kuroda Motoki]], "Takato Yoritsugu," *Takeda Shingen Encyclopedia* * Tanaka Toyoshige, *Shinano Medieval Samurai Biographies,* [[Shinano Mainichi Newspaper]], 2016. * [https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%AB%8F%E8%A8%AA%E9%A0%BC%E7%B6%99-85402 Kotobank: Suwa Yoritsugu]
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Takato Tsugimune|ja|高遠継宗}} * {{ill|Takato Yorimune (Renpōken)|ja|高遠頼宗}}

His posthumous name was 大用普徹大禅定院 (Daiyō Futtetsu Daizendō-in). Following his death, {{ill|Hoshina Masatoshi|ja|保科正俊}}, a vassal of the Takato clan, rose to prominence under the Takeda. While traditionally believed to mark the Takato clan's extinction, recent studies suggest that {{ill|Takeda Katsuyori|ja|武田勝頼}}, Takeda Shingen's fourth son, inherited the Takato-Suwa lineage.<ref>Marushima Kazuhiro, "Kōyasan Jōkei-in's *Kōkoku Kako-chō*: The *Past Records of Kōshū Monthly Memorials*," *Takeda Clan Studies,* No. 34, 2006.</ref>

revision.text:
'''Takato Yoritsugu''' (高遠 頼継, birth year unknown – [[Tenbun]] 21, [[August 16 (lunar calendar)|August 16]] ([[September 4]], [[1552]])?) was a {{ill|Samurai|ja|武士}} during Japan’s {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代 (日本)}}. He was a local lord ([[kokujin]]) of {{ill|Shinano Province|ja|信濃国}}, serving as the lord of {{ill|Takato Castle|ja|高遠城}} (located in present-day {{ill|Ina, Nagano|ja|伊那市}}, {{ill|Takato Town|ja|高遠町}}). As a retainer of the {{ill|Takeda clan|ja|甲斐武田氏}}, he held the position of {{ill|Sakikata-shū|ja|信濃先方衆}} in charge of the Shinano frontlines. He belonged to the {{ill|Takato clan|ja|高遠氏}}, a cadet branch of the {{ill|Suwa clan|ja|諏訪氏}}, and sometimes used the Suwa surname. His official titles included Shinano-no-Kami and Kii-no-Kami. Yoritsugu was the son and heir of Takato Mitsunari (Shinano-no-Kami) and married the daughter of {{ill|Suwa Yorimitsu (Aki-no-Kami)|ja|諏訪頼満 (安芸守)}}.
== Biography == Yoritsugu is said to have been the son or grandson of {{ill|Takato Tsugimune|ja|高遠継宗}}, who opposed the Suwa main family during internal conflicts. After {{ill|Suwa Yorimitsu (Aki-no-Kami)|ja|諏訪頼満}} unified the Suwa clan, Yoritsugu initially resisted but eventually became a vassal and married Yorimitsu’s daughter.
During the Sengoku period, the Takeda clan of {{ill|Kai Province|ja|甲斐国}} and the Suwa clan of Shinano Province had a long-standing alliance. However, in Tenbun 10 (1542), {{ill|Takeda Harunobu (Shingen)|ja|武田晴信}} (later known as Takeda Shingen) ascended as the Takeda clan head and began an aggressive campaign to conquer Shinano, breaking the alliance and invading the Suwa domain. On [[July 2]], Tenbun 11 (1542), Yoritsugu assisted the Takeda in attacking {{ill|Uehara Castle|ja|上原城}}, the base of {{ill|Suwa Yorishige|ja|諏訪頼重}}, who surrendered to the Takeda. Yorishige was taken to {{ill|Kōfu|ja|甲府}} and forced to commit suicide later that month. The Suwa domain was divided between the Takeda and Yoritsugu, with Yoritsugu controlling the western half.
Yoritsugu, aiming to restore the Suwa clan's authority, allied with {{ill|Fujisawa Yorichika|ja|藤沢頼親}} of {{ill|Fukuyo Castle|ja|福与城}} in {{ill|Ina District, Nagano|ja|伊那郡}} to invade Takeda territory. However, on [[September 25]], he was defeated in the {{ill|Battle of Miyagawa (1542)|ja|宮川の戦い}} and forced to retreat from the Suwa region.
In Tenbun 14 (1545), Takeda forces attacked the allied Fujisawa Yorichika and {{ill|Ōi Sadataka|ja|大井貞隆}} of {{ill|Nagakubo Castle|ja|長窪城}}. Takato Castle fell to the Takeda on [[April 17]], and Yoritsugu surrendered, traveling to Kōfu to submit to Takeda authority. After the castle’s capture, the Takeda fortified Takato Castle as a strategic base for controlling Shinano.
In Tenbun 17 (1548), after the Takeda’s defeat at the {{ill|Battle of Uedahara|ja|上田原の戦い}} against {{ill|Murakami Yoshikiyo|ja|村上義清}}, unrest spread in Takeda-controlled territories. Yoritsugu returned to Takato Castle on [[April 3]] (as noted in the *Kōhakusai-ki*).
Yoritsugu continued serving the Takeda but was reportedly forced to commit suicide during a campaign in {{ill|Shimoinadani|ja|下伊那}} in Tenbun 21 (1552). His posthumous name was 大用普徹大禅定院 (Daiyō Futtetsu Daizendō-in). Following his death, {{ill|Hoshina Masatoshi|ja|保科正俊}}, a vassal of the Takato clan, rose to prominence under the Takeda. While traditionally believed to mark the Takato clan's extinction, recent studies suggest that {{ill|Takeda Katsuyori|ja|武田勝頼}}, Takeda Shingen's fourth son, inherited the Takato-Suwa lineage.<ref>Marushima Kazuhiro, "Kōyasan Jōkei-in's *Kōkoku Kako-chō*: The *Past Records of Kōshū Monthly Memorials*," *Takeda Clan Studies,* No. 34, 2006.</ref>
== References == {{reflist}}
== Sources == * [[Kuroda Motoki]], "Takato Yoritsugu," *Takeda Shingen Encyclopedia* * Tanaka Toyoshige, *Shinano Medieval Samurai Biographies,* [[Shinano Mainichi Newspaper]], 2016. * [https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%AB%8F%E8%A8%AA%E9%A0%BC%E7%B6%99-85402 Kotobank: Suwa Yoritsugu]

revision.text:
'''Takato Yoritsugu''' (高遠 頼継, birth year unknown – [[Tenbun]] 21, [[August 16 (lunar calendar)|August 16]] ([[September 4]], [[1552]])?) was a {{ill|Samurai|ja|武士}} during Japan’s {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代 (日本)}}. He was a local lord ([[kokujin]]) of {{ill|Shinano Province|ja|信濃国}}, serving as the lord of {{ill|Takato Castle|ja|高遠城}} (located in present-day {{ill|Ina, Nagano|ja|伊那市}}, {{ill|Takato Town|ja|高遠町}}). As a retainer of the {{ill|Takeda clan|ja|甲斐武田氏}}, he held the position of {{ill|Sakikata-shū|ja|信濃先方衆}} in charge of the Shinano frontlines. He belonged to the {{ill|Takato clan|ja|高遠氏}}, a cadet branch of the {{ill|Suwa clan|ja|諏訪氏}}, and sometimes used the Suwa surname. His official titles included Shinano-no-Kami and Kii-no-Kami. Yoritsugu was the son and heir of Takato Mitsunari (Shinano-no-Kami) and married the daughter of {{ill|Suwa Yorimitsu (Aki-no-Kami)|ja|諏訪頼満 (安芸守)}}.
== Biography == Yoritsugu is said to have been the son or grandson of {{ill|Takato Tsugimune|ja|高遠継宗}}, who opposed the Suwa main family during internal conflicts. After {{ill|Suwa Yorimitsu (Aki-no-Kami)|ja|諏訪頼満}} unified the Suwa clan, Yoritsugu initially resisted but eventually became a vassal and married Yorimitsu’s daughter.
During the Sengoku period, the Takeda clan of {{ill|Kai Province|ja|甲斐国}} and the Suwa clan of Shinano Province had a long-standing alliance. However, in Tenbun 10 (1542), {{ill|Takeda Harunobu (Shingen)|ja|武田晴信}} (later known as Takeda Shingen) ascended as the Takeda clan head and began an aggressive campaign to conquer Shinano, breaking the alliance and invading the Suwa domain. On [[July 2]], Tenbun 11 (1542), Yoritsugu assisted the Takeda in attacking {{ill|Uehara Castle|ja|上原城}}, the base of {{ill|Suwa Yorishige|ja|諏訪頼重}}, who surrendered to the Takeda. Yorishige was taken to {{ill|Kōfu|ja|甲府}} and forced to commit suicide later that month. The Suwa domain was divided between the Takeda and Yoritsugu, with Yoritsugu controlling the western half.
Yoritsugu, aiming to restore the Suwa clan's authority, allied with {{ill|Fujisawa Yorichika|ja|藤沢頼親}} of {{ill|Fukuyo Castle|ja|福与城}} in {{ill|Ina District, Nagano|ja|伊那郡}} to invade Takeda territory. However, on [[September 25]], he was defeated in the {{ill|Battle of Miyagawa (1542)|ja|宮川の戦い}} and forced to retreat from the Suwa region.
In Tenbun 14 (1545), Takeda forces attacked the allied Fujisawa Yorichika and {{ill|Ōi Sadataka|ja|大井貞隆}} of {{ill|Nagakubo Castle|ja|長窪城}}. Takato Castle fell to the Takeda on [[April 17]], and Yoritsugu surrendered, traveling to Kōfu to submit to Takeda authority. After the castle’s capture, the Takeda fortified Takato Castle as a strategic base for controlling Shinano.
In Tenbun 17 (1548), after the Takeda’s defeat at the {{ill|Battle of Uedahara|ja|上田原の戦い}} against {{ill|Murakami Yoshikiyo|ja|村上義清}}, unrest spread in Takeda-controlled territories. Yoritsugu returned to Takato Castle on [[April 3]] (as noted in the *Kōhakusai-ki*).
Yoritsugu continued serving the Takeda but was reportedly forced to commit suicide during a campaign in {{ill|Shimoinadani|ja|下伊那}} in Tenbun 21 (1552). His posthumous name was 大用普徹大禅定院 (Daiyō Futtetsu Daizendō-in). Following his death, {{ill|Hoshina Masatoshi|ja|保科正俊}}, a vassal of the Takato clan, rose to prominence under the Takeda. While traditionally believed to mark the Takato clan's extinction, recent studies suggest that {{ill|Takeda Katsuyori|ja|武田勝頼}}, Takeda Shingen's fourth son, inherited the Takato-Suwa lineage.<ref>Marushima Kazuhiro, "Kōyasan Jōkei-in's *Kōkoku Kako-chō*: The *Past Records of Kōshū Monthly Memorials*," *Takeda Clan Studies,* No. 34, 2006.</ref>
== References == {{reflist}}
== Sources == * [[Kuroda Motoki]], "Takato Yoritsugu," *Takeda Shingen Encyclopedia* * Tanaka Toyoshige, *Shinano Medieval Samurai Biographies,* [[Shinano Mainichi Newspaper]], 2016. * [https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%AB%8F%E8%A8%AA%E9%A0%BC%E7%B6%99-85402 Kotobank: Suwa Yoritsugu]
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Takato Tsugimune|ja|高遠継宗}} * {{ill|Takato Yorimune (Renpōken)|ja|高遠頼宗}}

On [[July 2]], Tenbun 11 (1542), Yoritsugu assisted the Takeda in attacking {{ill|Uehara Castle|ja|上原城}}, the base of {{ill|Suwa Yorishige|ja|諏訪頼重}}, who surrendered to the Takeda. Yorishige was taken to {{ill|Kōfu|ja|甲府}} and forced to commit suicide later that month. The Suwa domain was divided between the Takeda and Yoritsugu, with Yoritsugu controlling the western half.
Yoritsugu, aiming to restore the Suwa clan's authority, allied with {{ill|Fujisawa Yorichika|ja|藤沢頼親}} of {{ill|Fukuyo Castle|ja|福与城}} in {{ill|Ina District, Nagano|ja|伊那郡}} to invade Takeda territory. However, on [[September 25]], he was defeated in the {{ill|Battle of Miyagawa (1542)|ja|宮川の戦い}} and forced to retreat from the Suwa region.
In Tenbun 14 (1545), Takeda forces attacked the allied Fujisawa Yorichika and {{ill|Ōi Sadataka|ja|大井貞隆}} of {{ill|Nagakubo Castle|ja|長窪城}}. Takato Castle fell to the Takeda on [[April 17]], and Yoritsugu surrendered, traveling to Kōfu to submit to Takeda authority. After the castle’s capture, the Takeda fortified Takato Castle as a strategic base for controlling Shinano.
In Tenbun 17 (1548), after the Takeda’s defeat at the {{ill|Battle of Uedahara|ja|上田原の戦い}} against {{ill|Murakami Yoshikiyo|ja|村上義清}}, unrest spread in Takeda-controlled territories. Yoritsugu returned to Takato Castle on [[April 3]] (as noted in the *Kōhakusai-ki*).
Yoritsugu continued serving the Takeda but was reportedly forced to commit suicide during a campaign in {{ill|Shimoinadani|ja|下伊那}} in Tenbun 21 (1552). His posthumous name was 大用普徹大禅定院 (Daiyō Futtetsu Daizendō-in). Following his death, {{ill|Hoshina Masatoshi|ja|保科正俊}}, a vassal of the Takato clan, rose to prominence under the Takeda. While traditionally believed to mark the Takato clan's extinction, recent studies suggest that {{ill|Takeda Katsuyori|ja|武田勝頼}}, Takeda Shingen's fourth son, inherited the Takato-Suwa lineage.<ref>Marushima Kazuhiro, "Kōyasan Jōkei-in's *Kōkoku Kako-chō*: The *Past Records of Kōshū Monthly Memorials*," *Takeda Clan Studies,* No. 34, 2006.</ref>

His posthumous name was 大用普徹大禅定院 (Daiyō Futtetsu Daizendō-in). Following his death, {{ill|Hoshina Masatoshi|ja|保科正俊}}, a vassal of the Takato clan, rose to prominence under the Takeda. While traditionally believed to mark the Takato clan's extinction, recent studies suggest that {{ill|Takeda Katsuyori|ja|武田勝頼}}, Takeda Shingen's fourth son, inherited the Takato-Suwa lineage.<ref>Marushima Kazuhiro, "Kōyasan Jōkei-in's *Kōkoku Kako-chō*: The *Past Records of Kōshū Monthly Memorials*," *Takeda Clan Studies,* No. 34, 2006.</ref>

revision.text:
'''Takato Yoritsugu''' (高遠 頼継, birth year unknown – [[Tenbun]] 21, [[August 16 (lunar calendar)|August 16]] ([[September 4]], [[1552]])?) was a {{ill|Samurai|ja|武士}} during Japan’s {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代 (日本)}}. He was a local lord ([[kokujin]]) of {{ill|Shinano Province|ja|信濃国}}, serving as the lord of {{ill|Takato Castle|ja|高遠城}} (located in present-day {{ill|Ina, Nagano|ja|伊那市}}, {{ill|Takato Town|ja|高遠町}}). As a retainer of the {{ill|Takeda clan|ja|甲斐武田氏}}, he held the position of {{ill|Sakikata-shū|ja|信濃先方衆}} in charge of the Shinano frontlines. He belonged to the {{ill|Takato clan|ja|高遠氏}}, a cadet branch of the {{ill|Suwa clan|ja|諏訪氏}}, and sometimes used the Suwa surname. His official titles included Shinano-no-Kami and Kii-no-Kami. Yoritsugu was the son and heir of Takato Mitsunari (Shinano-no-Kami) and married the daughter of {{ill|Suwa Yorimitsu (Aki-no-Kami)|ja|諏訪頼満 (安芸守)}}.
== Biography == Yoritsugu is said to have been the son or grandson of {{ill|Takato Tsugimune|ja|高遠継宗}}, who opposed the Suwa main family during internal conflicts. After {{ill|Suwa Yorimitsu (Aki-no-Kami)|ja|諏訪頼満}} unified the Suwa clan, Yoritsugu initially resisted but eventually became a vassal and married Yorimitsu’s daughter.
During the Sengoku period, the Takeda clan of {{ill|Kai Province|ja|甲斐国}} and the Suwa clan of Shinano Province had a long-standing alliance. However, in Tenbun 10 (1542), {{ill|Takeda Harunobu (Shingen)|ja|武田晴信}} (later known as Takeda Shingen) ascended as the Takeda clan head and began an aggressive campaign to conquer Shinano, breaking the alliance and invading the Suwa domain. On [[July 2]], Tenbun 11 (1542), Yoritsugu assisted the Takeda in attacking {{ill|Uehara Castle|ja|上原城}}, the base of {{ill|Suwa Yorishige|ja|諏訪頼重}}, who surrendered to the Takeda. Yorishige was taken to {{ill|Kōfu|ja|甲府}} and forced to commit suicide later that month. The Suwa domain was divided between the Takeda and Yoritsugu, with Yoritsugu controlling the western half.
Yoritsugu, aiming to restore the Suwa clan's authority, allied with {{ill|Fujisawa Yorichika|ja|藤沢頼親}} of {{ill|Fukuyo Castle|ja|福与城}} in {{ill|Ina District, Nagano|ja|伊那郡}} to invade Takeda territory. However, on [[September 25]], he was defeated in the {{ill|Battle of Miyagawa (1542)|ja|宮川の戦い}} and forced to retreat from the Suwa region.
In Tenbun 14 (1545), Takeda forces attacked the allied Fujisawa Yorichika and {{ill|Ōi Sadataka|ja|大井貞隆}} of {{ill|Nagakubo Castle|ja|長窪城}}. Takato Castle fell to the Takeda on [[April 17]], and Yoritsugu surrendered, traveling to Kōfu to submit to Takeda authority. After the castle’s capture, the Takeda fortified Takato Castle as a strategic base for controlling Shinano.
In Tenbun 17 (1548), after the Takeda’s defeat at the {{ill|Battle of Uedahara|ja|上田原の戦い}} against {{ill|Murakami Yoshikiyo|ja|村上義清}}, unrest spread in Takeda-controlled territories. Yoritsugu returned to Takato Castle on [[April 3]] (as noted in the *Kōhakusai-ki*).
Yoritsugu continued serving the Takeda but was reportedly forced to commit suicide during a campaign in {{ill|Shimoinadani|ja|下伊那}} in Tenbun 21 (1552). His posthumous name was 大用普徹大禅定院 (Daiyō Futtetsu Daizendō-in). Following his death, {{ill|Hoshina Masatoshi|ja|保科正俊}}, a vassal of the Takato clan, rose to prominence under the Takeda. While traditionally believed to mark the Takato clan's extinction, recent studies suggest that {{ill|Takeda Katsuyori|ja|武田勝頼}}, Takeda Shingen's fourth son, inherited the Takato-Suwa lineage.<ref>Marushima Kazuhiro, "Kōyasan Jōkei-in's *Kōkoku Kako-chō*: The *Past Records of Kōshū Monthly Memorials*," *Takeda Clan Studies,* No. 34, 2006.</ref>

revision.text:
'''Takato Yoritsugu''' (高遠 頼継, birth year unknown – [[Tenbun]] 21, [[August 16 (lunar calendar)|August 16]] ([[September 4]], [[1552]])?) was a {{ill|Samurai|ja|武士}} during Japan’s {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代 (日本)}}. He was a local lord ([[kokujin]]) of {{ill|Shinano Province|ja|信濃国}}, serving as the lord of {{ill|Takato Castle|ja|高遠城}} (located in present-day {{ill|Ina, Nagano|ja|伊那市}}, {{ill|Takato Town|ja|高遠町}}). As a retainer of the {{ill|Takeda clan|ja|甲斐武田氏}}, he held the position of {{ill|Sakikata-shū|ja|信濃先方衆}} in charge of the Shinano frontlines. He belonged to the {{ill|Takato clan|ja|高遠氏}}, a cadet branch of the {{ill|Suwa clan|ja|諏訪氏}}, and sometimes used the Suwa surname. His official titles included Shinano-no-Kami and Kii-no-Kami. Yoritsugu was the son and heir of Takato Mitsunari (Shinano-no-Kami) and married the daughter of {{ill|Suwa Yorimitsu (Aki-no-Kami)|ja|諏訪頼満 (安芸守)}}.
== Biography == Yoritsugu is said to have been the son or grandson of {{ill|Takato Tsugimune|ja|高遠継宗}}, who opposed the Suwa main family during internal conflicts. After {{ill|Suwa Yorimitsu (Aki-no-Kami)|ja|諏訪頼満}} unified the Suwa clan, Yoritsugu initially resisted but eventually became a vassal and married Yorimitsu’s daughter.
During the Sengoku period, the Takeda clan of {{ill|Kai Province|ja|甲斐国}} and the Suwa clan of Shinano Province had a long-standing alliance. However, in Tenbun 10 (1542), {{ill|Takeda Harunobu (Shingen)|ja|武田晴信}} (later known as Takeda Shingen) ascended as the Takeda clan head and began an aggressive campaign to conquer Shinano, breaking the alliance and invading the Suwa domain. On [[July 2]], Tenbun 11 (1542), Yoritsugu assisted the Takeda in attacking {{ill|Uehara Castle|ja|上原城}}, the base of {{ill|Suwa Yorishige|ja|諏訪頼重}}, who surrendered to the Takeda. Yorishige was taken to {{ill|Kōfu|ja|甲府}} and forced to commit suicide later that month. The Suwa domain was divided between the Takeda and Yoritsugu, with Yoritsugu controlling the western half.
Yoritsugu, aiming to restore the Suwa clan's authority, allied with {{ill|Fujisawa Yorichika|ja|藤沢頼親}} of {{ill|Fukuyo Castle|ja|福与城}} in {{ill|Ina District, Nagano|ja|伊那郡}} to invade Takeda territory. However, on [[September 25]], he was defeated in the {{ill|Battle of Miyagawa (1542)|ja|宮川の戦い}} and forced to retreat from the Suwa region.
In Tenbun 14 (1545), Takeda forces attacked the allied Fujisawa Yorichika and {{ill|Ōi Sadataka|ja|大井貞隆}} of {{ill|Nagakubo Castle|ja|長窪城}}. Takato Castle fell to the Takeda on [[April 17]], and Yoritsugu surrendered, traveling to Kōfu to submit to Takeda authority. After the castle’s capture, the Takeda fortified Takato Castle as a strategic base for controlling Shinano.
In Tenbun 17 (1548), after the Takeda’s defeat at the {{ill|Battle of Uedahara|ja|上田原の戦い}} against {{ill|Murakami Yoshikiyo|ja|村上義清}}, unrest spread in Takeda-controlled territories. Yoritsugu returned to Takato Castle on [[April 3]] (as noted in the *Kōhakusai-ki*).
Yoritsugu continued serving the Takeda but was reportedly forced to commit suicide during a campaign in {{ill|Shimoinadani|ja|下伊那}} in Tenbun 21 (1552). His posthumous name was 大用普徹大禅定院 (Daiyō Futtetsu Daizendō-in). Following his death, {{ill|Hoshina Masatoshi|ja|保科正俊}}, a vassal of the Takato clan, rose to prominence under the Takeda. While traditionally believed to mark the Takato clan's extinction, recent studies suggest that {{ill|Takeda Katsuyori|ja|武田勝頼}}, Takeda Shingen's fourth son, inherited the Takato-Suwa lineage.<ref>Marushima Kazuhiro, "Kōyasan Jōkei-in's *Kōkoku Kako-chō*: The *Past Records of Kōshū Monthly Memorials*," *Takeda Clan Studies,* No. 34, 2006.</ref>

revision.text:
'''Takato Yoritsugu''' (高遠 頼継, birth year unknown – [[Tenbun]] 21, [[August 16 (lunar calendar)|August 16]] ([[September 4]], [[1552]])?) was a {{ill|Samurai|ja|武士}} during Japan’s {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代 (日本)}}. He was a local lord ([[kokujin]]) of {{ill|Shinano Province|ja|信濃国}}, serving as the lord of {{ill|Takato Castle|ja|高遠城}} (located in present-day {{ill|Ina, Nagano|ja|伊那市}}, {{ill|Takato Town|ja|高遠町}}). As a retainer of the {{ill|Takeda clan|ja|甲斐武田氏}}, he held the position of {{ill|Sakikata-shū|ja|信濃先方衆}} in charge of the Shinano frontlines. He belonged to the {{ill|Takato clan|ja|高遠氏}}, a cadet branch of the {{ill|Suwa clan|ja|諏訪氏}}, and sometimes used the Suwa surname. His official titles included Shinano-no-Kami and Kii-no-Kami. Yoritsugu was the son and heir of Takato Mitsunari (Shinano-no-Kami) and married the daughter of {{ill|Suwa Yorimitsu (Aki-no-Kami)|ja|諏訪頼満 (安芸守)}}.
== Biography == Yoritsugu is said to have been the son or grandson of {{ill|Takato Tsugimune|ja|高遠継宗}}, who opposed the Suwa main family during internal conflicts. After {{ill|Suwa Yorimitsu (Aki-no-Kami)|ja|諏訪頼満}} unified the Suwa clan, Yoritsugu initially resisted but eventually became a vassal and married Yorimitsu’s daughter.
During the Sengoku period, the Takeda clan of {{ill|Kai Province|ja|甲斐国}} and the Suwa clan of Shinano Province had a long-standing alliance. However, in Tenbun 10 (1542), {{ill|Takeda Harunobu (Shingen)|ja|武田晴信}} (later known as Takeda Shingen) ascended as the Takeda clan head and began an aggressive campaign to conquer Shinano, breaking the alliance and invading the Suwa domain. On [[July 2]], Tenbun 11 (1542), Yoritsugu assisted the Takeda in attacking {{ill|Uehara Castle|ja|上原城}}, the base of {{ill|Suwa Yorishige|ja|諏訪頼重}}, who surrendered to the Takeda. Yorishige was taken to {{ill|Kōfu|ja|甲府}} and forced to commit suicide later that month. The Suwa domain was divided between the Takeda and Yoritsugu, with Yoritsugu controlling the western half.
Yoritsugu, aiming to restore the Suwa clan's authority, allied with {{ill|Fujisawa Yorichika|ja|藤沢頼親}} of {{ill|Fukuyo Castle|ja|福与城}} in {{ill|Ina District, Nagano|ja|伊那郡}} to invade Takeda territory. However, on [[September 25]], he was defeated in the {{ill|Battle of Miyagawa (1542)|ja|宮川の戦い}} and forced to retreat from the Suwa region.
In Tenbun 14 (1545), Takeda forces attacked the allied Fujisawa Yorichika and {{ill|Ōi Sadataka|ja|大井貞隆}} of {{ill|Nagakubo Castle|ja|長窪城}}. Takato Castle fell to the Takeda on [[April 17]], and Yoritsugu surrendered, traveling to Kōfu to submit to Takeda authority. After the castle’s capture, the Takeda fortified Takato Castle as a strategic base for controlling Shinano.
In Tenbun 17 (1548), after the Takeda’s defeat at the {{ill|Battle of Uedahara|ja|上田原の戦い}} against {{ill|Murakami Yoshikiyo|ja|村上義清}}, unrest spread in Takeda-controlled territories. Yoritsugu returned to Takato Castle on [[April 3]] (as noted in the *Kōhakusai-ki*).
Yoritsugu continued serving the Takeda but was reportedly forced to commit suicide during a campaign in {{ill|Shimoinadani|ja|下伊那}} in Tenbun 21 (1552). His posthumous name was 大用普徹大禅定院 (Daiyō Futtetsu Daizendō-in). Following his death, {{ill|Hoshina Masatoshi|ja|保科正俊}}, a vassal of the Takato clan, rose to prominence under the Takeda. While traditionally believed to mark the Takato clan's extinction, recent studies suggest that {{ill|Takeda Katsuyori|ja|武田勝頼}}, Takeda Shingen's fourth son, inherited the Takato-Suwa lineage.<ref>Marushima Kazuhiro, "Kōyasan Jōkei-in's *Kōkoku Kako-chō*: The *Past Records of Kōshū Monthly Memorials*," *Takeda Clan Studies,* No. 34, 2006.</ref>
== References == {{reflist}}
== Sources == * [[Kuroda Motoki]], "Takato Yoritsugu," *Takeda Shingen Encyclopedia* * Tanaka Toyoshige, *Shinano Medieval Samurai Biographies,* [[Shinano Mainichi Newspaper]], 2016. * [https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%AB%8F%E8%A8%AA%E9%A0%BC%E7%B6%99-85402 Kotobank: Suwa Yoritsugu]
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Takato Tsugimune|ja|高遠継宗}} * {{ill|Takato Yorimune (Renpōken)|ja|高遠頼宗}}

He was a local lord ([[kokujin]]) of {{ill|Shinano Province|ja|信濃国}}, serving as the lord of {{ill|Takato Castle|ja|高遠城}} (located in present-day {{ill|Ina, Nagano|ja|伊那市}}, {{ill|Takato Town|ja|高遠町}}). As a retainer of the {{ill|Takeda clan|ja|甲斐武田氏}}, he held the position of {{ill|Sakikata-shū|ja|信濃先方衆}} in charge of the Shinano frontlines. He belonged to the {{ill|Takato clan|ja|高遠氏}}, a cadet branch of the {{ill|Suwa clan|ja|諏訪氏}}, and sometimes used the Suwa surname. His official titles included Shinano-no-Kami and Kii-no-Kami. Yoritsugu was the son and heir of Takato Mitsunari (Shinano-no-Kami) and married the daughter of {{ill|Suwa Yorimitsu (Aki-no-Kami)|ja|諏訪頼満 (安芸守)}}.
== Biography == Yoritsugu is said to have been the son or grandson of {{ill|Takato Tsugimune|ja|高遠継宗}}, who opposed the Suwa main family during internal conflicts. After {{ill|Suwa Yorimitsu (Aki-no-Kami)|ja|諏訪頼満}} unified the Suwa clan, Yoritsugu initially resisted but eventually became a vassal and married Yorimitsu’s daughter.
During the Sengoku period, the Takeda clan of {{ill|Kai Province|ja|甲斐国}} and the Suwa clan of Shinano Province had a long-standing alliance. However, in Tenbun 10 (1542), {{ill|Takeda Harunobu (Shingen)|ja|武田晴信}} (later known as Takeda Shingen) ascended as the Takeda clan head and began an aggressive campaign to conquer Shinano, breaking the alliance and invading the Suwa domain. On [[July 2]], Tenbun 11 (1542), Yoritsugu assisted the Takeda in attacking {{ill|Uehara Castle|ja|上原城}}, the base of {{ill|Suwa Yorishige|ja|諏訪頼重}}, who surrendered to the Takeda. Yorishige was taken to {{ill|Kōfu|ja|甲府}} and forced to commit suicide later that month. The Suwa domain was divided between the Takeda and Yoritsugu, with Yoritsugu controlling the western half.
Yoritsugu, aiming to restore the Suwa clan's authority, allied with {{ill|Fujisawa Yorichika|ja|藤沢頼親}} of {{ill|Fukuyo Castle|ja|福与城}} in {{ill|Ina District, Nagano|ja|伊那郡}} to invade Takeda territory. However, on [[September 25]], he was defeated in the {{ill|Battle of Miyagawa (1542)|ja|宮川の戦い}} and forced to retreat from the Suwa region.
In Tenbun 14 (1545), Takeda forces attacked the allied Fujisawa Yorichika and {{ill|Ōi Sadataka|ja|大井貞隆}} of {{ill|Nagakubo Castle|ja|長窪城}}. Takato Castle fell to the Takeda on [[April 17]], and Yoritsugu surrendered, traveling to Kōfu to submit to Takeda authority. After the castle’s capture, the Takeda fortified Takato Castle as a strategic base for controlling Shinano.
In Tenbun 17 (1548), after the Takeda’s defeat at the {{ill|Battle of Uedahara|ja|上田原の戦い}} against {{ill|Murakami Yoshikiyo|ja|村上義清}}, unrest spread in Takeda-controlled territories. Yoritsugu returned to Takato Castle on [[April 3]] (as noted in the *Kōhakusai-ki*).
Yoritsugu continued serving the Takeda but was reportedly forced to commit suicide during a campaign in {{ill|Shimoinadani|ja|下伊那}} in Tenbun 21 (1552). His posthumous name was 大用普徹大禅定院 (Daiyō Futtetsu Daizendō-in). Following his death, {{ill|Hoshina Masatoshi|ja|保科正俊}}, a vassal of the Takato clan, rose to prominence under the Takeda. While traditionally believed to mark the Takato clan's extinction, recent studies suggest that {{ill|Takeda Katsuyori|ja|武田勝頼}}, Takeda Shingen's fourth son, inherited the Takato-Suwa lineage.<ref>Marushima Kazuhiro, "Kōyasan Jōkei-in's *Kōkoku Kako-chō*: The *Past Records of Kōshū Monthly Memorials*," *Takeda Clan Studies,* No. 34, 2006.</ref>

'''Takato Yoritsugu''' (高遠 頼継, birth year unknown – [[Tenbun]] 21, [[August 16 (lunar calendar)|August 16]] ([[September 4]], [[1552]])?) was a {{ill|Samurai|ja|武士}} during Japan’s {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代 (日本)}}. He was a local lord ([[kokujin]]) of {{ill|Shinano Province|ja|信濃国}}, serving as the lord of {{ill|Takato Castle|ja|高遠城}} (located in present-day {{ill|Ina, Nagano|ja|伊那市}}, {{ill|Takato Town|ja|高遠町}}). As a retainer of the {{ill|Takeda clan|ja|甲斐武田氏}}, he held the position of {{ill|Sakikata-shū|ja|信濃先方衆}} in charge of the Shinano frontlines. He belonged to the {{ill|Takato clan|ja|高遠氏}}, a cadet branch of the {{ill|Suwa clan|ja|諏訪氏}}, and sometimes used the Suwa surname. His official titles included Shinano-no-Kami and Kii-no-Kami. Yoritsugu was the son and heir of Takato Mitsunari (Shinano-no-Kami) and married the daughter of {{ill|Suwa Yorimitsu (Aki-no-Kami)|ja|諏訪頼満 (安芸守)}}.
== Biography == Yoritsugu is said to have been the son or grandson of {{ill|Takato Tsugimune|ja|高遠継宗}}, who opposed the Suwa main family during internal conflicts. After {{ill|Suwa Yorimitsu (Aki-no-Kami)|ja|諏訪頼満}} unified the Suwa clan, Yoritsugu initially resisted but eventually became a vassal and married Yorimitsu’s daughter.
During the Sengoku period, the Takeda clan of {{ill|Kai Province|ja|甲斐国}} and the Suwa clan of Shinano Province had a long-standing alliance. However, in Tenbun 10 (1542), {{ill|Takeda Harunobu (Shingen)|ja|武田晴信}} (later known as Takeda Shingen) ascended as the Takeda clan head and began an aggressive campaign to conquer Shinano, breaking the alliance and invading the Suwa domain. On [[July 2]], Tenbun 11 (1542), Yoritsugu assisted the Takeda in attacking {{ill|Uehara Castle|ja|上原城}}, the base of {{ill|Suwa Yorishige|ja|諏訪頼重}}, who surrendered to the Takeda. Yorishige was taken to {{ill|Kōfu|ja|甲府}} and forced to commit suicide later that month. The Suwa domain was divided between the Takeda and Yoritsugu, with Yoritsugu controlling the western half.
Yoritsugu, aiming to restore the Suwa clan's authority, allied with {{ill|Fujisawa Yorichika|ja|藤沢頼親}} of {{ill|Fukuyo Castle|ja|福与城}} in {{ill|Ina District, Nagano|ja|伊那郡}} to invade Takeda territory. However, on [[September 25]], he was defeated in the {{ill|Battle of Miyagawa (1542)|ja|宮川の戦い}} and forced to retreat from the Suwa region.
In Tenbun 14 (1545), Takeda forces attacked the allied Fujisawa Yorichika and {{ill|Ōi Sadataka|ja|大井貞隆}} of {{ill|Nagakubo Castle|ja|長窪城}}. Takato Castle fell to the Takeda on [[April 17]], and Yoritsugu surrendered, traveling to Kōfu to submit to Takeda authority. After the castle’s capture, the Takeda fortified Takato Castle as a strategic base for controlling Shinano.
In Tenbun 17 (1548), after the Takeda’s defeat at the {{ill|Battle of Uedahara|ja|上田原の戦い}} against {{ill|Murakami Yoshikiyo|ja|村上義清}}, unrest spread in Takeda-controlled territories. Yoritsugu returned to Takato Castle on [[April 3]] (as noted in the *Kōhakusai-ki*).
Yoritsugu continued serving the Takeda but was reportedly forced to commit suicide during a campaign in {{ill|Shimoinadani|ja|下伊那}} in Tenbun 21 (1552). His posthumous name was 大用普徹大禅定院 (Daiyō Futtetsu Daizendō-in). Following his death, {{ill|Hoshina Masatoshi|ja|保科正俊}}, a vassal of the Takato clan, rose to prominence under the Takeda. While traditionally believed to mark the Takato clan's extinction, recent studies suggest that {{ill|Takeda Katsuyori|ja|武田勝頼}}, Takeda Shingen's fourth son, inherited the Takato-Suwa lineage.<ref>Marushima Kazuhiro, "Kōyasan Jōkei-in's *Kōkoku Kako-chō*: The *Past Records of Kōshū Monthly Memorials*," *Takeda Clan Studies,* No. 34, 2006.</ref>
== References == {{reflist}}
== Sources == * [[Kuroda Motoki]], "Takato Yoritsugu," *Takeda Shingen Encyclopedia* * Tanaka Toyoshige, *Shinano Medieval Samurai Biographies,* [[Shinano Mainichi Newspaper]], 2016. * [https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%AB%8F%E8%A8%AA%E9%A0%BC%E7%B6%99-85402 Kotobank: Suwa Yoritsugu]

revision.comment: mainspace to namespace
revision.text:
'''Takato Yoritsugu''' (高遠 頼継, birth year unknown – [[Tenbun]] 21, [[August 16 (lunar calendar)|August 16]] ([[September 4]], [[1552]])?) was a {{ill|Samurai|ja|武士}} during Japan’s {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代 (日本)}}. He was a local lord ([[kokujin]]) of {{ill|Shinano Province|ja|信濃国}}, serving as the lord of {{ill|Takato Castle|ja|高遠城}} (located in present-day {{ill|Ina, Nagano|ja|伊那市}}, {{ill|Takato Town|ja|高遠町}}). As a retainer of the {{ill|Takeda clan|ja|甲斐武田氏}}, he held the position of {{ill|Sakikata-shū|ja|信濃先方衆}} in charge of the Shinano frontlines. He belonged to the {{ill|Takato clan|ja|高遠氏}}, a cadet branch of the {{ill|Suwa clan|ja|諏訪氏}}, and sometimes used the Suwa surname. His official titles included Shinano-no-Kami and Kii-no-Kami. Yoritsugu was the son and heir of Takato Mitsunari (Shinano-no-Kami) and married the daughter of {{ill|Suwa Yorimitsu (Aki-no-Kami)|ja|諏訪頼満 (安芸守)}}.
== Biography == Yoritsugu is said to have been the son or grandson of {{ill|Takato Tsugimune|ja|高遠継宗}}, who opposed the Suwa main family during internal conflicts. After {{ill|Suwa Yorimitsu (Aki-no-Kami)|ja|諏訪頼満}} unified the Suwa clan, Yoritsugu initially resisted but eventually became a vassal and married Yorimitsu’s daughter.
During the Sengoku period, the Takeda clan of {{ill|Kai Province|ja|甲斐国}} and the Suwa clan of Shinano Province had a long-standing alliance. However, in Tenbun 10 (1542), {{ill|Takeda Harunobu (Shingen)|ja|武田晴信}} (later known as Takeda Shingen) ascended as the Takeda clan head and began an aggressive campaign to conquer Shinano, breaking the alliance and invading the Suwa domain. On [[July 2]], Tenbun 11 (1542), Yoritsugu assisted the Takeda in attacking {{ill|Uehara Castle|ja|上原城}}, the base of {{ill|Suwa Yorishige|ja|諏訪頼重}}, who surrendered to the Takeda. Yorishige was taken to {{ill|Kōfu|ja|甲府}} and forced to commit suicide later that month. The Suwa domain was divided between the Takeda and Yoritsugu, with Yoritsugu controlling the western half.
Yoritsugu, aiming to restore the Suwa clan's authority, allied with {{ill|Fujisawa Yorichika|ja|藤沢頼親}} of {{ill|Fukuyo Castle|ja|福与城}} in {{ill|Ina District, Nagano|ja|伊那郡}} to invade Takeda territory. However, on [[September 25]], he was defeated in the {{ill|Battle of Miyagawa (1542)|ja|宮川の戦い}} and forced to retreat from the Suwa region.
In Tenbun 14 (1545), Takeda forces attacked the allied Fujisawa Yorichika and {{ill|Ōi Sadataka|ja|大井貞隆}} of {{ill|Nagakubo Castle|ja|長窪城}}. Takato Castle fell to the Takeda on [[April 17]], and Yoritsugu surrendered, traveling to Kōfu to submit to Takeda authority. After the castle’s capture, the Takeda fortified Takato Castle as a strategic base for controlling Shinano.
In Tenbun 17 (1548), after the Takeda’s defeat at the {{ill|Battle of Uedahara|ja|上田原の戦い}} against {{ill|Murakami Yoshikiyo|ja|村上義清}}, unrest spread in Takeda-controlled territories. Yoritsugu returned to Takato Castle on [[April 3]] (as noted in the *Kōhakusai-ki*).
Yoritsugu continued serving the Takeda but was reportedly forced to commit suicide during a campaign in {{ill|Shimoinadani|ja|下伊那}} in Tenbun 21 (1552). His posthumous name was 大用普徹大禅定院 (Daiyō Futtetsu Daizendō-in). Following his death, {{ill|Hoshina Masatoshi|ja|保科正俊}}, a vassal of the Takato clan, rose to prominence under the Takeda. While traditionally believed to mark the Takato clan's extinction, recent studies suggest that {{ill|Takeda Katsuyori|ja|武田勝頼}}, Takeda Shingen's fourth son, inherited the Takato-Suwa lineage.<ref>Marushima Kazuhiro, "Kōyasan Jōkei-in's *Kōkoku Kako-chō*: The *Past Records of Kōshū Monthly Memorials*," *Takeda Clan Studies,* No. 34, 2006.</ref>
== References == {{reflist}}
== Sources == * [[Kuroda Motoki]], "Takato Yoritsugu," *Takeda Shingen Encyclopedia* * Tanaka Toyoshige, *Shinano Medieval Samurai Biographies,* [[Shinano Mainichi Newspaper]], 2016. * [https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%AB%8F%E8%A8%AA%E9%A0%BC%E7%B6%99-85402 Kotobank: Suwa Yoritsugu]
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Takato Tsugimune|ja|高遠継宗}} * {{ill|Takato Yorimune (Renpōken)|ja|高遠頼宗}}

While traditionally believed to mark the Takato clan's extinction, recent studies suggest that {{ill|Takeda Katsuyori|ja|武田勝頼}}, Takeda Shingen's fourth son, inherited the Takato-Suwa lineage.<ref>Marushima Kazuhiro, "Kōyasan Jōkei-in's *Kōkoku Kako-chō*: The *Past Records of Kōshū Monthly Memorials*," *Takeda Clan Studies,* No. 34, 2006.</ref>

'''Takato Yoritsugu''' (高遠 頼継, birth year unknown – [[Tenbun]] 21, [[August 16 (lunar calendar)|August 16]] ([[September 4]], [[1552]])?) was a {{ill|Samurai|ja|武士}} during Japan’s {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代 (日本)}}. He was a local lord ([[kokujin]]) of {{ill|Shinano Province|ja|信濃国}}, serving as the lord of {{ill|Takato Castle|ja|高遠城}} (located in present-day {{ill|Ina, Nagano|ja|伊那市}}, {{ill|Takato Town|ja|高遠町}}). As a retainer of the {{ill|Takeda clan|ja|甲斐武田氏}}, he held the position of {{ill|Sakikata-shū|ja|信濃先方衆}} in charge of the Shinano frontlines. He belonged to the {{ill|Takato clan|ja|高遠氏}}, a cadet branch of the {{ill|Suwa clan|ja|諏訪氏}}, and sometimes used the Suwa surname. His official titles included Shinano-no-Kami and Kii-no-Kami. Yoritsugu was the son and heir of Takato Mitsunari (Shinano-no-Kami) and married the daughter of {{ill|Suwa Yorimitsu (Aki-no-Kami)|ja|諏訪頼満 (安芸守)}}.
== Biography == Yoritsugu is said to have been the son or grandson of {{ill|Takato Tsugimune|ja|高遠継宗}}, who opposed the Suwa main family during internal conflicts. After {{ill|Suwa Yorimitsu (Aki-no-Kami)|ja|諏訪頼満}} unified the Suwa clan, Yoritsugu initially resisted but eventually became a vassal and married Yorimitsu’s daughter.
During the Sengoku period, the Takeda clan of {{ill|Kai Province|ja|甲斐国}} and the Suwa clan of Shinano Province had a long-standing alliance. However, in Tenbun 10 (1542), {{ill|Takeda Harunobu (Shingen)|ja|武田晴信}} (later known as Takeda Shingen) ascended as the Takeda clan head and began an aggressive campaign to conquer Shinano, breaking the alliance and invading the Suwa domain. On [[July 2]], Tenbun 11 (1542), Yoritsugu assisted the Takeda in attacking {{ill|Uehara Castle|ja|上原城}}, the base of {{ill|Suwa Yorishige|ja|諏訪頼重}}, who surrendered to the Takeda. Yorishige was taken to {{ill|Kōfu|ja|甲府}} and forced to commit suicide later that month. The Suwa domain was divided between the Takeda and Yoritsugu, with Yoritsugu controlling the western half.
Yoritsugu, aiming to restore the Suwa clan's authority, allied with {{ill|Fujisawa Yorichika|ja|藤沢頼親}} of {{ill|Fukuyo Castle|ja|福与城}} in {{ill|Ina District, Nagano|ja|伊那郡}} to invade Takeda territory. However, on [[September 25]], he was defeated in the {{ill|Battle of Miyagawa (1542)|ja|宮川の戦い}} and forced to retreat from the Suwa region.
In Tenbun 14 (1545), Takeda forces attacked the allied Fujisawa Yorichika and {{ill|Ōi Sadataka|ja|大井貞隆}} of {{ill|Nagakubo Castle|ja|長窪城}}. Takato Castle fell to the Takeda on [[April 17]], and Yoritsugu surrendered, traveling to Kōfu to submit to Takeda authority. After the castle’s capture, the Takeda fortified Takato Castle as a strategic base for controlling Shinano.
In Tenbun 17 (1548), after the Takeda’s defeat at the {{ill|Battle of Uedahara|ja|上田原の戦い}} against {{ill|Murakami Yoshikiyo|ja|村上義清}}, unrest spread in Takeda-controlled territories. Yoritsugu returned to Takato Castle on [[April 3]] (as noted in the *Kōhakusai-ki*).
Yoritsugu continued serving the Takeda but was reportedly forced to commit suicide during a campaign in {{ill|Shimoinadani|ja|下伊那}} in Tenbun 21 (1552). His posthumous name was 大用普徹大禅定院 (Daiyō Futtetsu Daizendō-in). Following his death, {{ill|Hoshina Masatoshi|ja|保科正俊}}, a vassal of the Takato clan, rose to prominence under the Takeda. While traditionally believed to mark the Takato clan's extinction, recent studies suggest that {{ill|Takeda Katsuyori|ja|武田勝頼}}, Takeda Shingen's fourth son, inherited the Takato-Suwa lineage.<ref>Marushima Kazuhiro, "Kōyasan Jōkei-in's *Kōkoku Kako-chō*: The *Past Records of Kōshū Monthly Memorials*," *Takeda Clan Studies,* No. 34, 2006.</ref>

Yoritsugu, aiming to restore the Suwa clan's authority, allied with {{ill|Fujisawa Yorichika|ja|藤沢頼親}} of {{ill|Fukuyo Castle|ja|福与城}} in {{ill|Ina District, Nagano|ja|伊那郡}} to invade Takeda territory. However, on [[September 25]], he was defeated in the {{ill|Battle of Miyagawa (1542)|ja|宮川の戦い}} and forced to retreat from the Suwa region.
In Tenbun 14 (1545), Takeda forces attacked the allied Fujisawa Yorichika and {{ill|Ōi Sadataka|ja|大井貞隆}} of {{ill|Nagakubo Castle|ja|長窪城}}. Takato Castle fell to the Takeda on [[April 17]], and Yoritsugu surrendered, traveling to Kōfu to submit to Takeda authority. After the castle’s capture, the Takeda fortified Takato Castle as a strategic base for controlling Shinano.
In Tenbun 17 (1548), after the Takeda’s defeat at the {{ill|Battle of Uedahara|ja|上田原の戦い}} against {{ill|Murakami Yoshikiyo|ja|村上義清}}, unrest spread in Takeda-controlled territories. Yoritsugu returned to Takato Castle on [[April 3]] (as noted in the *Kōhakusai-ki*).
Yoritsugu continued serving the Takeda but was reportedly forced to commit suicide during a campaign in {{ill|Shimoinadani|ja|下伊那}} in Tenbun 21 (1552). His posthumous name was 大用普徹大禅定院 (Daiyō Futtetsu Daizendō-in). Following his death, {{ill|Hoshina Masatoshi|ja|保科正俊}}, a vassal of the Takato clan, rose to prominence under the Takeda. While traditionally believed to mark the Takato clan's extinction, recent studies suggest that {{ill|Takeda Katsuyori|ja|武田勝頼}}, Takeda Shingen's fourth son, inherited the Takato-Suwa lineage.<ref>Marushima Kazuhiro, "Kōyasan Jōkei-in's *Kōkoku Kako-chō*: The *Past Records of Kōshū Monthly Memorials*," *Takeda Clan Studies,* No. 34, 2006.</ref>

'''Takato Yoritsugu''' (高遠 頼継, birth year unknown – [[Tenbun]] 21, [[August 16 (lunar calendar)|August 16]] ([[September 4]], [[1552]])?) was a {{ill|Samurai|ja|武士}} during Japan’s {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代 (日本)}}. He was a local lord ([[kokujin]]) of {{ill|Shinano Province|ja|信濃国}}, serving as the lord of {{ill|Takato Castle|ja|高遠城}} (located in present-day {{ill|Ina, Nagano|ja|伊那市}}, {{ill|Takato Town|ja|高遠町}}). As a retainer of the {{ill|Takeda clan|ja|甲斐武田氏}}, he held the position of {{ill|Sakikata-shū|ja|信濃先方衆}} in charge of the Shinano frontlines. He belonged to the {{ill|Takato clan|ja|高遠氏}}, a cadet branch of the {{ill|Suwa clan|ja|諏訪氏}}, and sometimes used the Suwa surname. His official titles included Shinano-no-Kami and Kii-no-Kami. Yoritsugu was the son and heir of Takato Mitsunari (Shinano-no-Kami) and married the daughter of {{ill|Suwa Yorimitsu (Aki-no-Kami)|ja|諏訪頼満 (安芸守)}}.
== Biography == Yoritsugu is said to have been the son or grandson of {{ill|Takato Tsugimune|ja|高遠継宗}}, who opposed the Suwa main family during internal conflicts. After {{ill|Suwa Yorimitsu (Aki-no-Kami)|ja|諏訪頼満}} unified the Suwa clan, Yoritsugu initially resisted but eventually became a vassal and married Yorimitsu’s daughter.
During the Sengoku period, the Takeda clan of {{ill|Kai Province|ja|甲斐国}} and the Suwa clan of Shinano Province had a long-standing alliance. However, in Tenbun 10 (1542), {{ill|Takeda Harunobu (Shingen)|ja|武田晴信}} (later known as Takeda Shingen) ascended as the Takeda clan head and began an aggressive campaign to conquer Shinano, breaking the alliance and invading the Suwa domain. On [[July 2]], Tenbun 11 (1542), Yoritsugu assisted the Takeda in attacking {{ill|Uehara Castle|ja|上原城}}, the base of {{ill|Suwa Yorishige|ja|諏訪頼重}}, who surrendered to the Takeda. Yorishige was taken to {{ill|Kōfu|ja|甲府}} and forced to commit suicide later that month. The Suwa domain was divided between the Takeda and Yoritsugu, with Yoritsugu controlling the western half.
Yoritsugu, aiming to restore the Suwa clan's authority, allied with {{ill|Fujisawa Yorichika|ja|藤沢頼親}} of {{ill|Fukuyo Castle|ja|福与城}} in {{ill|Ina District, Nagano|ja|伊那郡}} to invade Takeda territory. However, on [[September 25]], he was defeated in the {{ill|Battle of Miyagawa (1542)|ja|宮川の戦い}} and forced to retreat from the Suwa region.
In Tenbun 14 (1545), Takeda forces attacked the allied Fujisawa Yorichika and {{ill|Ōi Sadataka|ja|大井貞隆}} of {{ill|Nagakubo Castle|ja|長窪城}}. Takato Castle fell to the Takeda on [[April 17]], and Yoritsugu surrendered, traveling to Kōfu to submit to Takeda authority. After the castle’s capture, the Takeda fortified Takato Castle as a strategic base for controlling Shinano.
In Tenbun 17 (1548), after the Takeda’s defeat at the {{ill|Battle of Uedahara|ja|上田原の戦い}} against {{ill|Murakami Yoshikiyo|ja|村上義清}}, unrest spread in Takeda-controlled territories. Yoritsugu returned to Takato Castle on [[April 3]] (as noted in the *Kōhakusai-ki*).
Yoritsugu continued serving the Takeda but was reportedly forced to commit suicide during a campaign in {{ill|Shimoinadani|ja|下伊那}} in Tenbun 21 (1552). His posthumous name was 大用普徹大禅定院 (Daiyō Futtetsu Daizendō-in). Following his death, {{ill|Hoshina Masatoshi|ja|保科正俊}}, a vassal of the Takato clan, rose to prominence under the Takeda. While traditionally believed to mark the Takato clan's extinction, recent studies suggest that {{ill|Takeda Katsuyori|ja|武田勝頼}}, Takeda Shingen's fourth son, inherited the Takato-Suwa lineage.<ref>Marushima Kazuhiro, "Kōyasan Jōkei-in's *Kōkoku Kako-chō*: The *Past Records of Kōshū Monthly Memorials*," *Takeda Clan Studies,* No. 34, 2006.</ref>

revision.comment: Text replacement - "—>" to "-->"
revision.text:
'''Takato Yoritsugu''' (高遠 頼継, birth year unknown – [[Tenbun]] 21, [[August 16 (lunar calendar)|August 16]] ([[September 4]], [[1552]])?) was a {{ill|Samurai|ja|武士}} during Japan’s {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代 (日本)}}. He was a local lord ([[kokujin]]) of {{ill|Shinano Province|ja|信濃国}}, serving as the lord of {{ill|Takato Castle|ja|高遠城}} (located in present-day {{ill|Ina, Nagano|ja|伊那市}}, {{ill|Takato Town|ja|高遠町}}). As a retainer of the {{ill|Takeda clan|ja|甲斐武田氏}}, he held the position of {{ill|Sakikata-shū|ja|信濃先方衆}} in charge of the Shinano frontlines. He belonged to the {{ill|Takato clan|ja|高遠氏}}, a cadet branch of the {{ill|Suwa clan|ja|諏訪氏}}, and sometimes used the Suwa surname. His official titles included Shinano-no-Kami and Kii-no-Kami. Yoritsugu was the son and heir of Takato Mitsunari (Shinano-no-Kami) and married the daughter of {{ill|Suwa Yorimitsu (Aki-no-Kami)|ja|諏訪頼満 (安芸守)}}.
== Biography == Yoritsugu is said to have been the son or grandson of {{ill|Takato Tsugimune|ja|高遠継宗}}, who opposed the Suwa main family during internal conflicts. After {{ill|Suwa Yorimitsu (Aki-no-Kami)|ja|諏訪頼満}} unified the Suwa clan, Yoritsugu initially resisted but eventually became a vassal and married Yorimitsu’s daughter.
During the Sengoku period, the Takeda clan of {{ill|Kai Province|ja|甲斐国}} and the Suwa clan of Shinano Province had a long-standing alliance. However, in Tenbun 10 (1542), {{ill|Takeda Harunobu (Shingen)|ja|武田晴信}} (later known as Takeda Shingen) ascended as the Takeda clan head and began an aggressive campaign to conquer Shinano, breaking the alliance and invading the Suwa domain. On [[July 2]], Tenbun 11 (1542), Yoritsugu assisted the Takeda in attacking {{ill|Uehara Castle|ja|上原城}}, the base of {{ill|Suwa Yorishige|ja|諏訪頼重}}, who surrendered to the Takeda. Yorishige was taken to {{ill|Kōfu|ja|甲府}} and forced to commit suicide later that month. The Suwa domain was divided between the Takeda and Yoritsugu, with Yoritsugu controlling the western half.
Yoritsugu, aiming to restore the Suwa clan's authority, allied with {{ill|Fujisawa Yorichika|ja|藤沢頼親}} of {{ill|Fukuyo Castle|ja|福与城}} in {{ill|Ina District, Nagano|ja|伊那郡}} to invade Takeda territory. However, on [[September 25]], he was defeated in the {{ill|Battle of Miyagawa (1542)|ja|宮川の戦い}} and forced to retreat from the Suwa region.
In Tenbun 14 (1545), Takeda forces attacked the allied Fujisawa Yorichika and {{ill|Ōi Sadataka|ja|大井貞隆}} of {{ill|Nagakubo Castle|ja|長窪城}}. Takato Castle fell to the Takeda on [[April 17]], and Yoritsugu surrendered, traveling to Kōfu to submit to Takeda authority. After the castle’s capture, the Takeda fortified Takato Castle as a strategic base for controlling Shinano.
In Tenbun 17 (1548), after the Takeda’s defeat at the {{ill|Battle of Uedahara|ja|上田原の戦い}} against {{ill|Murakami Yoshikiyo|ja|村上義清}}, unrest spread in Takeda-controlled territories. Yoritsugu returned to Takato Castle on [[April 3]] (as noted in the *Kōhakusai-ki*).
Yoritsugu continued serving the Takeda but was reportedly forced to commit suicide during a campaign in {{ill|Shimoinadani|ja|下伊那}} in Tenbun 21 (1552). His posthumous name was 大用普徹大禅定院 (Daiyō Futtetsu Daizendō-in). Following his death, {{ill|Hoshina Masatoshi|ja|保科正俊}}, a vassal of the Takato clan, rose to prominence under the Takeda. While traditionally believed to mark the Takato clan's extinction, recent studies suggest that {{ill|Takeda Katsuyori|ja|武田勝頼}}, Takeda Shingen's fourth son, inherited the Takato-Suwa lineage.<ref>Marushima Kazuhiro, "Kōyasan Jōkei-in's *Kōkoku Kako-chō*: The *Past Records of Kōshū Monthly Memorials*," *Takeda Clan Studies,* No. 34, 2006.</ref>
== References == {{reflist}}
== Sources == * [[Kuroda Motoki]], "Takato Yoritsugu," *Takeda Shingen Encyclopedia* * Tanaka Toyoshige, *Shinano Medieval Samurai Biographies,* [[Shinano Mainichi Newspaper]], 2016. * [https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%AB%8F%E8%A8%AA%E9%A0%BC%E7%B6%99-85402 Kotobank: Suwa Yoritsugu]
== See Also == * {{ill|Takato Tsugimune|ja|高遠継宗}} * {{ill|Takato Yorimune (Renpōken)|ja|高遠頼宗}}

However, in Tenbun 10 (1542), {{ill|Takeda Harunobu (Shingen)|ja|武田晴信}} (later known as Takeda Shingen) ascended as the Takeda clan head and began an aggressive campaign to conquer Shinano, breaking the alliance and invading the Suwa domain. On [[July 2]], Tenbun 11 (1542), Yoritsugu assisted the Takeda in attacking {{ill|Uehara Castle|ja|上原城}}, the base of {{ill|Suwa Yorishige|ja|諏訪頼重}}, who surrendered to the Takeda. Yorishige was taken to {{ill|Kōfu|ja|甲府}} and forced to commit suicide later that month. The Suwa domain was divided between the Takeda and Yoritsugu, with Yoritsugu controlling the western half.
Yoritsugu, aiming to restore the Suwa clan's authority, allied with {{ill|Fujisawa Yorichika|ja|藤沢頼親}} of {{ill|Fukuyo Castle|ja|福与城}} in {{ill|Ina District, Nagano|ja|伊那郡}} to invade Takeda territory. However, on [[September 25]], he was defeated in the {{ill|Battle of Miyagawa (1542)|ja|宮川の戦い}} and forced to retreat from the Suwa region.
In Tenbun 14 (1545), Takeda forces attacked the allied Fujisawa Yorichika and {{ill|Ōi Sadataka|ja|大井貞隆}} of {{ill|Nagakubo Castle|ja|長窪城}}. Takato Castle fell to the Takeda on [[April 17]], and Yoritsugu surrendered, traveling to Kōfu to submit to Takeda authority. After the castle’s capture, the Takeda fortified Takato Castle as a strategic base for controlling Shinano.
In Tenbun 17 (1548), after the Takeda’s defeat at the {{ill|Battle of Uedahara|ja|上田原の戦い}} against {{ill|Murakami Yoshikiyo|ja|村上義清}}, unrest spread in Takeda-controlled territories. Yoritsugu returned to Takato Castle on [[April 3]] (as noted in the *Kōhakusai-ki*).
Yoritsugu continued serving the Takeda but was reportedly forced to commit suicide during a campaign in {{ill|Shimoinadani|ja|下伊那}} in Tenbun 21 (1552). His posthumous name was 大用普徹大禅定院 (Daiyō Futtetsu Daizendō-in). Following his death, {{ill|Hoshina Masatoshi|ja|保科正俊}}, a vassal of the Takato clan, rose to prominence under the Takeda. While traditionally believed to mark the Takato clan's extinction, recent studies suggest that {{ill|Takeda Katsuyori|ja|武田勝頼}}, Takeda Shingen's fourth son, inherited the Takato-Suwa lineage.<ref>Marushima Kazuhiro, "Kōyasan Jōkei-in's *Kōkoku Kako-chō*: The *Past Records of Kōshū Monthly Memorials*," *Takeda Clan Studies,* No. 34, 2006.</ref>

'''Takato Yoritsugu''' (高遠 頼継, birth year unknown – [[Tenbun]] 21, [[August 16 (lunar calendar)|August 16]] ([[September 4]], [[1552]])?) was a {{ill|Samurai|ja|武士}} during Japan’s {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代 (日本)}}. He was a local lord ([[kokujin]]) of {{ill|Shinano Province|ja|信濃国}}, serving as the lord of {{ill|Takato Castle|ja|高遠城}} (located in present-day {{ill|Ina, Nagano|ja|伊那市}}, {{ill|Takato Town|ja|高遠町}}). As a retainer of the {{ill|Takeda clan|ja|甲斐武田氏}}, he held the position of {{ill|Sakikata-shū|ja|信濃先方衆}} in charge of the Shinano frontlines. He belonged to the {{ill|Takato clan|ja|高遠氏}}, a cadet branch of the {{ill|Suwa clan|ja|諏訪氏|1=Suwa clan}}, and sometimes used the Suwa surname. His official titles included Shinano-no-Kami and Kii-no-Kami. Yoritsugu was the son and heir of Takato Mitsunari (Shinano-no-Kami) and married the daughter of {{ill|Suwa Yorimitsu (Aki-no-Kami)|ja|諏訪頼満 (安芸守)}}.
== Biography == Yoritsugu is said to have been the son or grandson of {{ill|Takato Tsugimune|ja|高遠継宗}}, who opposed the Suwa main family during internal conflicts. After {{ill|Suwa Yorimitsu (Aki-no-Kami)|ja|諏訪頼満}} unified the Suwa clan, Yoritsugu initially resisted but eventually became a vassal and married Yorimitsu’s daughter.
During the Sengoku period, the Takeda clan of {{ill|Kai Province|ja|甲斐国}} and the Suwa clan of Shinano Province had a long-standing alliance. However, in Tenbun 10 (1542), {{ill|Takeda Harunobu (Shingen)|ja|武田晴信}} (later known as Takeda Shingen) ascended as the Takeda clan head and began an aggressive campaign to conquer Shinano, breaking the alliance and invading the Suwa domain. On [[July 2]], Tenbun 11 (1542), Yoritsugu assisted the Takeda in attacking {{ill|Uehara Castle|ja|上原城}}, the base of {{ill|Suwa Yorishige|ja|諏訪頼重}}, who surrendered to the Takeda. Yorishige was taken to {{ill|Kōfu|ja|甲府}} and forced to commit suicide later that month. The Suwa domain was divided between the Takeda and Yoritsugu, with Yoritsugu controlling the western half.
Yoritsugu, aiming to restore the Suwa clan's authority, allied with {{ill|Fujisawa Yorichika|ja|藤沢頼親}} of {{ill|Fukuyo Castle|ja|福与城}} in {{ill|Ina District, Nagano|ja|伊那郡}} to invade Takeda territory. However, on [[September 25]], he was defeated in the {{ill|Battle of Miyagawa (1542)|ja|宮川の戦い}} and forced to retreat from the Suwa region.
In Tenbun 14 (1545), Takeda forces attacked the allied Fujisawa Yorichika and {{ill|Ōi Sadataka|ja|大井貞隆}} of {{ill|Nagakubo Castle|ja|長窪城}}. Takato Castle fell to the Takeda on [[April 17]], and Yoritsugu surrendered, traveling to Kōfu to submit to Takeda authority. After the castle’s capture, the Takeda fortified Takato Castle as a strategic base for controlling Shinano.
In Tenbun 17 (1548), after the Takeda’s defeat at the {{ill|Battle of Uedahara|ja|上田原の戦い}} against {{ill|Murakami Yoshikiyo|ja|村上義清}}, unrest spread in Takeda-controlled territories. Yoritsugu returned to Takato Castle on [[April 3]] (as noted in the *Kōhakusai-ki*).
Yoritsugu continued serving the Takeda but was reportedly forced to commit suicide during a campaign in {{ill|Shimoinadani|ja|下伊那}} in Tenbun 21 (1552). His posthumous name was 大用普徹大禅定院 (Daiyō Futtetsu Daizendō-in). Following his death, {{ill|Hoshina Masatoshi|ja|保科正俊}}, a vassal of the Takato clan, rose to prominence under the Takeda. While traditionally believed to mark the Takato clan's extinction, recent studies suggest that {{ill|Takeda Katsuyori|ja|武田勝頼}}, Takeda Shingen's fourth son, inherited the Takato-Suwa lineage.<ref>Marushima Kazuhiro, "Kōyasan Jōkei-in's *Kōkoku Kako-chō*: The *Past Records of Kōshū Monthly Memorials*," *Takeda Clan Studies,* No. 34, 2006.</ref>

revision.text:
'''Takato Yoritsugu''' (高遠 頼継, birth year unknown – [[Tenbun]] 21, [[August 16 (lunar calendar)|August 16]] ([[September 4]], [[1552]])?) was a {{ill|Samurai|ja|武士}} during Japan’s {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代 (日本)}}. He was a local lord ([[kokujin]]) of {{ill|Shinano Province|ja|信濃国}}, serving as the lord of {{ill|Takato Castle|ja|高遠城}} (located in present-day {{ill|Ina, Nagano|ja|伊那市}}, {{ill|Takato Town|ja|高遠町}}). As a retainer of the {{ill|Takeda clan|ja|甲斐武田氏}}, he held the position of {{ill|Sakikata-shū|ja|信濃先方衆}} in charge of the Shinano frontlines. He belonged to the {{ill|Takato clan|ja|高遠氏}}, a cadet branch of the {{ill|Suwa clan|ja|諏訪氏|1=Suwa clan}}, and sometimes used the Suwa surname. His official titles included Shinano-no-Kami and Kii-no-Kami. Yoritsugu was the son and heir of Takato Mitsunari (Shinano-no-Kami) and married the daughter of {{ill|Suwa Yorimitsu (Aki-no-Kami)|ja|諏訪頼満 (安芸守)}}.
== Biography == Yoritsugu is said to have been the son or grandson of {{ill|Takato Tsugimune|ja|高遠継宗}}, who opposed the Suwa main family during internal conflicts. After {{ill|Suwa Yorimitsu (Aki-no-Kami)|ja|諏訪頼満}} unified the Suwa clan, Yoritsugu initially resisted but eventually became a vassal and married Yorimitsu’s daughter.
During the Sengoku period, the Takeda clan of {{ill|Kai Province|ja|甲斐国}} and the Suwa clan of Shinano Province had a long-standing alliance. However, in Tenbun 10 (1542), {{ill|Takeda Harunobu (Shingen)|ja|武田晴信}} (later known as Takeda Shingen) ascended as the Takeda clan head and began an aggressive campaign to conquer Shinano, breaking the alliance and invading the Suwa domain. On [[July 2]], Tenbun 11 (1542), Yoritsugu assisted the Takeda in attacking {{ill|Uehara Castle|ja|上原城}}, the base of {{ill|Suwa Yorishige|ja|諏訪頼重}}, who surrendered to the Takeda. Yorishige was taken to {{ill|Kōfu|ja|甲府}} and forced to commit suicide later that month. The Suwa domain was divided between the Takeda and Yoritsugu, with Yoritsugu controlling the western half.
Yoritsugu, aiming to restore the Suwa clan's authority, allied with {{ill|Fujisawa Yorichika|ja|藤沢頼親}} of {{ill|Fukuyo Castle|ja|福与城}} in {{ill|Ina District, Nagano|ja|伊那郡}} to invade Takeda territory. However, on [[September 25]], he was defeated in the {{ill|Battle of Miyagawa (1542)|ja|宮川の戦い}} and forced to retreat from the Suwa region.
In Tenbun 14 (1545), Takeda forces attacked the allied Fujisawa Yorichika and {{ill|Ōi Sadataka|ja|大井貞隆}} of {{ill|Nagakubo Castle|ja|長窪城}}. Takato Castle fell to the Takeda on [[April 17]], and Yoritsugu surrendered, traveling to Kōfu to submit to Takeda authority. After the castle’s capture, the Takeda fortified Takato Castle as a strategic base for controlling Shinano.
In Tenbun 17 (1548), after the Takeda’s defeat at the {{ill|Battle of Uedahara|ja|上田原の戦い}} against {{ill|Murakami Yoshikiyo|ja|村上義清}}, unrest spread in Takeda-controlled territories. Yoritsugu returned to Takato Castle on [[April 3]] (as noted in the *Kōhakusai-ki*).
Yoritsugu continued serving the Takeda but was reportedly forced to commit suicide during a campaign in {{ill|Shimoinadani|ja|下伊那}} in Tenbun 21 (1552). His posthumous name was 大用普徹大禅定院 (Daiyō Futtetsu Daizendō-in). Following his death, {{ill|Hoshina Masatoshi|ja|保科正俊}}, a vassal of the Takato clan, rose to prominence under the Takeda. While traditionally believed to mark the Takato clan's extinction, recent studies suggest that {{ill|Takeda Katsuyori|ja|武田勝頼}}, Takeda Shingen's fourth son, inherited the Takato-Suwa lineage.<ref>Marushima Kazuhiro, "Kōyasan Jōkei-in's *Kōkoku Kako-chō*: The *Past Records of Kōshū Monthly Memorials*," *Takeda Clan Studies,* No. 34, 2006.</ref>
== References == {{reflist}}
== Sources == * [[Kuroda Motoki]], "Takato Yoritsugu," *Takeda Shingen Encyclopedia* * Tanaka Toyoshige, *Shinano Medieval Samurai Biographies,* [[Shinano Mainichi Newspaper]], 2016. * [https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%AB%8F%E8%A8%AA%E9%A0%BC%E7%B6%99-85402 Kotobank: Suwa Yoritsugu]
== See Also == * {{ill|Takato Tsugimune|ja|高遠継宗}} * {{ill|Takato Yorimune (Renpōken)|ja|高遠頼宗}}

In Tenbun 17 (1548), after the Takeda’s defeat at the {{ill|Battle of Uedahara|ja|上田原の戦い}} against {{ill|Murakami Yoshikiyo|ja|村上義清}}, unrest spread in Takeda-controlled territories. Yoritsugu returned to Takato Castle on [[April 3]] (as noted in the *Kōhakusai-ki*).
Yoritsugu continued serving the Takeda but was reportedly forced to commit suicide during a campaign in {{ill|Shimoinadani|ja|下伊那}} in Tenbun 21 (1552). His posthumous name was 大用普徹大禅定院 (Daiyō Futtetsu Daizendō-in). Following his death, {{ill|Hoshina Masatoshi|ja|保科正俊}}, a vassal of the Takato clan, rose to prominence under the Takeda. While traditionally believed to mark the Takato clan's extinction, recent studies suggest that {{ill|Takeda Katsuyori|ja|武田勝頼}}, Takeda Shingen's fourth son, inherited the Takato-Suwa lineage.<ref>Marushima Kazuhiro, "Kōyasan Jōkei-in's *Kōkoku Kako-chō*: The *Past Records of Kōshū Monthly Memorials*," *Takeda Clan Studies,* No. 34, 2006.</ref>
