revision.text:
'''Takato Yoritsugu''' (高遠 頼継, birth year unknown – [[:en:Tenbun|Tenbun]] 21, [[August 16 (lunar calendar)|August 16]] ([[:en:September 4|September 4]], [[:en:1552|1552]])?) was a {{ill|Samurai|ja|武士|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Samurai|qq=}} during Japan’s [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]]. He was a local lord ([[:en:Kokujin|Kokujin]]) of [[:en:Shinano Province|Shinano Province]], serving as the lord of [[:en:Takatō Castle|Takato Castle]] (located in present-day [[:en:Ina, Nagano|Ina, Nagano]], [[:en:Takatō, Nagano|Takato Town]]). As a retainer of the {{ill|Takeda clan|ja|甲斐武田氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda clan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 武田氏}}, he held the position of {{ill|Sakikata-shū|ja|信濃先方衆|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakikata-shū|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in charge of the Shinano frontlines. He belonged to the {{ill|Takato clan|ja|高遠氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takato clan|qq=}}, a cadet branch of the [[:en:Suwa clan|Suwa clan]], and sometimes used the Suwa surname. His official titles included Shinano-no-Kami and Kii-no-Kami. Yoritsugu was the son and heir of Takato Mitsunari (Shinano-no-Kami) and married the daughter of [[:en:Suwa Yorimitsu|Suwa Yorimitsu (Aki-no-Kami)]].
== Biography == Yoritsugu is said to have been the son or grandson of {{ill|Takato Tsugimune|ja|高遠継宗|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takato Tsugimune|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who opposed the Suwa main family during internal conflicts. After {{ill|Suwa Yorimitsu (Aki-no-Kami)|ja|諏訪頼満|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Yorimitsu (Aki-no-Kami)|qq=}} unified the Suwa clan, Yoritsugu initially resisted but eventually became a vassal and married Yorimitsu’s daughter.
During the Sengoku period, the Takeda clan of [[:en:Kai Province|Kai Province]] and the Suwa clan of Shinano Province had a long-standing alliance. However, in Tenbun 10 (1542), {{ill|Takeda Harunobu (Shingen)|ja|武田晴信|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Harunobu (Shingen)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 武田信玄}} (later known as Takeda Shingen) ascended as the Takeda clan head and began an aggressive campaign to conquer Shinano, breaking the alliance and invading the Suwa domain. On [[:en:July 2|July 2]], Tenbun 11 (1542), Yoritsugu assisted the Takeda in attacking {{ill|Uehara Castle|ja|上原城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uehara Castle|qq=}}, the base of [[:en:Suwa Yorishige|Suwa Yorishige]], who surrendered to the Takeda. Yorishige was taken to {{ill|Kōfu|ja|甲府|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōfu|qq=}} and forced to commit suicide later that month. The Suwa domain was divided between the Takeda and Yoritsugu, with Yoritsugu controlling the western half.
Yoritsugu, aiming to restore the Suwa clan's authority, allied with {{ill|Fujisawa Yorichika|ja|藤沢頼親|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujisawa Yorichika|qq=}} of {{ill|Fukuyo Castle|ja|福与城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukuyo Castle|qq=}} in {{ill|Ina District, Nagano|ja|伊那郡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ina District, Nagano|qq=}} to invade Takeda territory. However, on [[:en:September 25|September 25]], he was defeated in the {{ill|Battle of Miyagawa (1542)|ja|宮川の戦い|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Battle of Miyagawa (1542)|qq=}} and forced to retreat from the Suwa region.
In Tenbun 14 (1545), Takeda forces attacked the allied Fujisawa Yorichika and {{ill|Ōi Sadataka|ja|大井貞隆|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Sadataka|qq=}} of {{ill|Nagakubo Castle|ja|長窪城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagakubo Castle|qq=}}. Takato Castle fell to the Takeda on [[:en:April 17|April 17]], and Yoritsugu surrendered, traveling to Kōfu to submit to Takeda authority. After the castle’s capture, the Takeda fortified Takato Castle as a strategic base for controlling Shinano.
In Tenbun 17 (1548), after the Takeda’s defeat at the [[:en:Battle of Uedahara|Battle of Uedahara]] against [[:en:Murakami Yoshikiyo|Murakami Yoshikiyo]], unrest spread in Takeda-controlled territories. Yoritsugu returned to Takato Castle on [[:en:April 3|April 3]] (as noted in the *Kōhakusai-ki*).
Yoritsugu continued serving the Takeda but was reportedly forced to commit suicide during a campaign in {{ill|Shimoinadani|ja|下伊那|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimoinadani|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 南信州地域}} in Tenbun 21 (1552). His posthumous name was 大用普徹大禅定院 (Daiyō Futtetsu Daizendō-in). Following his death, [[:en:Hoshina Masatoshi|Hoshina Masatoshi]], a vassal of the Takato clan, rose to prominence under the Takeda. While traditionally believed to mark the Takato clan's extinction, recent studies suggest that [[:en:Takeda Katsuyori|Takeda Katsuyori]], Takeda Shingen's fourth son, inherited the Takato-Suwa lineage.<ref>Marushima Kazuhiro, "Kōyasan Jōkei-in's *Kōkoku Kako-chō*: The *Past Records of Kōshū Monthly Memorials*," *Takeda Clan Studies,* No. 34, 2006.</ref>

However, on [[:en:September 25|September 25]], he was defeated in the {{ill|Battle of Miyagawa (1542)|ja|宮川の戦い|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Battle of Miyagawa (1542)|qq=}} and forced to retreat from the Suwa region.
In Tenbun 14 (1545), Takeda forces attacked the allied Fujisawa Yorichika and {{ill|Ōi Sadataka|ja|大井貞隆|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Sadataka|qq=}} of {{ill|Nagakubo Castle|ja|長窪城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagakubo Castle|qq=}}. Takato Castle fell to the Takeda on [[:en:April 17|April 17]], and Yoritsugu surrendered, traveling to Kōfu to submit to Takeda authority. After the castle’s capture, the Takeda fortified Takato Castle as a strategic base for controlling Shinano.
In Tenbun 17 (1548), after the Takeda’s defeat at the [[:en:Battle of Uedahara|Battle of Uedahara]] against [[:en:Murakami Yoshikiyo|Murakami Yoshikiyo]], unrest spread in Takeda-controlled territories. Yoritsugu returned to Takato Castle on [[:en:April 3|April 3]] (as noted in the *Kōhakusai-ki*).
Yoritsugu continued serving the Takeda but was reportedly forced to commit suicide during a campaign in {{ill|Shimoinadani|ja|下伊那|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimoinadani|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 南信州地域}} in Tenbun 21 (1552). His posthumous name was 大用普徹大禅定院 (Daiyō Futtetsu Daizendō-in). Following his death, [[:en:Hoshina Masatoshi|Hoshina Masatoshi]], a vassal of the Takato clan, rose to prominence under the Takeda. While traditionally believed to mark the Takato clan's extinction, recent studies suggest that [[:en:Takeda Katsuyori|Takeda Katsuyori]], Takeda Shingen's fourth son, inherited the Takato-Suwa lineage.<ref>Marushima Kazuhiro, "Kōyasan Jōkei-in's *Kōkoku Kako-chō*: The *Past Records of Kōshū Monthly Memorials*," *Takeda Clan Studies,* No. 34, 2006.</ref>

revision.comment: Bot: append |1= |comment= |WD= in {{ill}}
revision.text:
{{Infobox person|death_date=1552|gender={{ill|male|qid=Q6581097}}|occupation={{ill|military commander|qid=Q11545923}}|title={{ill|daimyo|qid=Q61982}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11672966]] Wikidata name: Takatō Yoritsugu [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:45, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Takato Yoritsugu''' (高遠 頼継, birth year unknown – [[:en:Tenbun|Tenbun]] 21, [[August 16 (lunar calendar)|August 16]] ([[:en:September 4|September 4]], [[:en:1552|1552]])?) was a {{ill|Samurai|ja|武士|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Samurai|qq=|1=bushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1975935}} during Japan’s [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]]. He was a local lord ([[:en:Kokujin|Kokujin]]) of [[:en:Shinano Province|Shinano Province]], serving as the lord of [[:en:Takatō Castle|Takato Castle]] (located in present-day [[:en:Ina, Nagano|Ina, Nagano]], [[:en:Takatō, Nagano|Takato Town]]). As a retainer of the {{ill|Takeda clan|ja|甲斐武田氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda clan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 武田氏}}, he held the position of {{ill|Sakikata-shū|ja|信濃先方衆|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakikata-shū|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in charge of the Shinano frontlines. He belonged to the {{ill|Takato clan|ja|高遠氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takato clan|qq=|1=Takato clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11672957}}, a cadet branch of the [[:en:Suwa clan|Suwa clan]], and sometimes used the Suwa surname. His official titles included Shinano-no-Kami and Kii-no-Kami. Yoritsugu was the son and heir of Takato Mitsunari (Shinano-no-Kami) and married the daughter of [[:en:Suwa Yorimitsu|Suwa Yorimitsu (Aki-no-Kami)]].
== Biography == Yoritsugu is said to have been the son or grandson of {{ill|Takato Tsugimune|ja|高遠継宗|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takato Tsugimune|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who opposed the Suwa main family during internal conflicts. After {{ill|Suwa Yorimitsu (Aki-no-Kami)|ja|諏訪頼満|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Yorimitsu (Aki-no-Kami)|qq=|1=Suwa Yorimitsu (Aki-no-Kami)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11631959}} unified the Suwa clan, Yoritsugu initially resisted but eventually became a vassal and married Yorimitsu’s daughter.
During the Sengoku period, the Takeda clan of [[:en:Kai Province|Kai Province]] and the Suwa clan of Shinano Province had a long-standing alliance. However, in Tenbun 10 (1542), {{ill|Takeda Harunobu (Shingen)|ja|武田晴信|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Harunobu (Shingen)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 武田信玄|1=Takeda Shingen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q276404}} (later known as Takeda Shingen) ascended as the Takeda clan head and began an aggressive campaign to conquer Shinano, breaking the alliance and invading the Suwa domain. On [[:en:July 2|July 2]], Tenbun 11 (1542), Yoritsugu assisted the Takeda in attacking {{ill|Uehara Castle|ja|上原城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uehara Castle|qq=|1=Uehara Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11358576}}, the base of [[:en:Suwa Yorishige|Suwa Yorishige]], who surrendered to the Takeda. Yorishige was taken to {{ill|Kōfu|ja|甲府|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōfu|qq=|1=Kōfu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11577765}} and forced to commit suicide later that month. The Suwa domain was divided between the Takeda and Yoritsugu, with Yoritsugu controlling the western half.
Yoritsugu, aiming to restore the Suwa clan's authority, allied with {{ill|Fujisawa Yorichika|ja|藤沢頼親|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujisawa Yorichika|qq=|1=Fujisawa Yorichika|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11624578}} of {{ill|Fukuyo Castle|ja|福与城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukuyo Castle|qq=|1=Fukuyo Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11591217}} in {{ill|Ina District, Nagano|ja|伊那郡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ina District, Nagano|qq=|1=Ina district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11380911}} to invade Takeda territory. However, on [[:en:September 25|September 25]], he was defeated in the {{ill|Battle of Miyagawa (1542)|ja|宮川の戦い|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Battle of Miyagawa (1542)|qq=|1=Battle of Miyagawa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17214872}} and forced to retreat from the Suwa region.
In Tenbun 14 (1545), Takeda forces attacked the allied Fujisawa Yorichika and {{ill|Ōi Sadataka|ja|大井貞隆|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Sadataka|qq=|1=Ōi Sadataka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11432233}} of {{ill|Nagakubo Castle|ja|長窪城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagakubo Castle|qq=|1=Nagakubo Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11653547}}. Takato Castle fell to the Takeda on [[:en:April 17|April 17]], and Yoritsugu surrendered, traveling to Kōfu to submit to Takeda authority. After the castle’s capture, the Takeda fortified Takato Castle as a strategic base for controlling Shinano.
In Tenbun 17 (1548), after the Takeda’s defeat at the [[:en:Battle of Uedahara|Battle of Uedahara]] against [[:en:Murakami Yoshikiyo|Murakami Yoshikiyo]], unrest spread in Takeda-controlled territories. Yoritsugu returned to Takato Castle on [[:en:April 3|April 3]] (as noted in the *Kōhakusai-ki*).
Yoritsugu continued serving the Takeda but was reportedly forced to commit suicide during a campaign in {{ill|Shimoinadani|ja|下伊那|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimoinadani|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 南信州地域|1=Minami Shinshū area|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11666112}} in Tenbun 21 (1552). His posthumous name was 大用普徹大禅定院 (Daiyō Futtetsu Daizendō-in). Following his death, [[:en:Hoshina Masatoshi|Hoshina Masatoshi]], a vassal of the Takato clan, rose to prominence under the Takeda. While traditionally believed to mark the Takato clan's extinction, recent studies suggest that [[:en:Takeda Katsuyori|Takeda Katsuyori]], Takeda Shingen's fourth son, inherited the Takato-Suwa lineage.<ref>Marushima Kazuhiro, "Kōyasan Jōkei-in's *Kōkoku Kako-chō*: The *Past Records of Kōshū Monthly Memorials*," *Takeda Clan Studies,* No. 34, 2006.</ref>

Yoritsugu was the son and heir of Takato Mitsunari (Shinano-no-Kami) and married the daughter of [[:en:Suwa Yorimitsu|Suwa Yorimitsu (Aki-no-Kami)]].
== Biography == Yoritsugu is said to have been the son or grandson of {{ill|Takato Tsugimune|ja|高遠継宗|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takato Tsugimune|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who opposed the Suwa main family during internal conflicts. After {{ill|Suwa Yorimitsu (Aki-no-Kami)|ja|諏訪頼満|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Yorimitsu (Aki-no-Kami)|qq=|1=Suwa Yorimitsu (Aki-no-Kami)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11631959}} unified the Suwa clan, Yoritsugu initially resisted but eventually became a vassal and married Yorimitsu’s daughter.
During the Sengoku period, the Takeda clan of [[:en:Kai Province|Kai Province]] and the Suwa clan of Shinano Province had a long-standing alliance. However, in Tenbun 10 (1542), {{ill|Takeda Harunobu (Shingen)|ja|武田晴信|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Harunobu (Shingen)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 武田信玄|1=Takeda Shingen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q276404}} (later known as Takeda Shingen) ascended as the Takeda clan head and began an aggressive campaign to conquer Shinano, breaking the alliance and invading the Suwa domain. On [[:en:July 2|July 2]], Tenbun 11 (1542), Yoritsugu assisted the Takeda in attacking {{ill|Uehara Castle|ja|上原城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uehara Castle|qq=|1=Uehara Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11358576}}, the base of [[:en:Suwa Yorishige|Suwa Yorishige]], who surrendered to the Takeda. Yorishige was taken to {{ill|Kōfu|ja|甲府|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōfu|qq=|1=Kōfu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11577765}} and forced to commit suicide later that month. The Suwa domain was divided between the Takeda and Yoritsugu, with Yoritsugu controlling the western half.
Yoritsugu, aiming to restore the Suwa clan's authority, allied with {{ill|Fujisawa Yorichika|ja|藤沢頼親|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujisawa Yorichika|qq=|1=Fujisawa Yorichika|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11624578}} of {{ill|Fukuyo Castle|ja|福与城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukuyo Castle|qq=|1=Fukuyo Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11591217}} in {{ill|Ina District, Nagano|ja|伊那郡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ina District, Nagano|qq=|1=Ina district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11380911}} to invade Takeda territory. However, on [[:en:September 25|September 25]], he was defeated in the {{ill|Battle of Miyagawa (1542)|ja|宮川の戦い|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Battle of Miyagawa (1542)|qq=|1=Battle of Miyagawa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17214872}} and forced to retreat from the Suwa region.
In Tenbun 14 (1545), Takeda forces attacked the allied Fujisawa Yorichika and {{ill|Ōi Sadataka|ja|大井貞隆|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Sadataka|qq=|1=Ōi Sadataka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11432233}} of {{ill|Nagakubo Castle|ja|長窪城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagakubo Castle|qq=|1=Nagakubo Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11653547}}. Takato Castle fell to the Takeda on [[:en:April 17|April 17]], and Yoritsugu surrendered, traveling to Kōfu to submit to Takeda authority. After the castle’s capture, the Takeda fortified Takato Castle as a strategic base for controlling Shinano.
In Tenbun 17 (1548), after the Takeda’s defeat at the [[:en:Battle of Uedahara|Battle of Uedahara]] against [[:en:Murakami Yoshikiyo|Murakami Yoshikiyo]], unrest spread in Takeda-controlled territories. Yoritsugu returned to Takato Castle on [[:en:April 3|April 3]] (as noted in the *Kōhakusai-ki*).
Yoritsugu continued serving the Takeda but was reportedly forced to commit suicide during a campaign in {{ill|Shimoinadani|ja|下伊那|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimoinadani|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 南信州地域|1=Minami Shinshū area|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11666112}} in Tenbun 21 (1552). His posthumous name was 大用普徹大禅定院 (Daiyō Futtetsu Daizendō-in). Following his death, [[:en:Hoshina Masatoshi|Hoshina Masatoshi]], a vassal of the Takato clan, rose to prominence under the Takeda. While traditionally believed to mark the Takato clan's extinction, recent studies suggest that [[:en:Takeda Katsuyori|Takeda Katsuyori]], Takeda Shingen's fourth son, inherited the Takato-Suwa lineage.<ref>Marushima Kazuhiro, "Kōyasan Jōkei-in's *Kōkoku Kako-chō*: The *Past Records of Kōshū Monthly Memorials*," *Takeda Clan Studies,* No. 34, 2006.</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:تاکاتوئو یوریتسوگو]][[it:Takatō Yoritsugu]][[ja:高遠頼継]][[zh:高遠賴繼]] {{Infobox person|death_date=1552|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|occupation={{ill|military commander|WD=Q11545923}}|title={{ill|daimyo|WD=Q61982}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11672966]] Wikidata name: Takatō Yoritsugu [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:45, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Takato Yoritsugu''' (高遠 頼継, birth year unknown – [[:en:Tenbun|Tenbun]] 21, [[August 16 (lunar calendar)|August 16]] ([[:en:September 4|September 4]], [[:en:1552|1552]])?) was a {{ill|Samurai|ja|武士|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Samurai|qq=|1=bushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1975935}} during Japan’s [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]]. He was a local lord ([[:en:Kokujin|Kokujin]]) of [[:en:Shinano Province|Shinano Province]], serving as the lord of [[:en:Takatō Castle|Takato Castle]] (located in present-day [[:en:Ina, Nagano|Ina, Nagano]], [[:en:Takatō, Nagano|Takato Town]]). As a retainer of the {{ill|Takeda clan|ja|甲斐武田氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda clan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 武田氏}}, he held the position of {{ill|Sakikata-shū|ja|信濃先方衆|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakikata-shū|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in charge of the Shinano frontlines. He belonged to the {{ill|Takato clan|ja|高遠氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takato clan|qq=|1=Takato clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11672957}}, a cadet branch of the [[:en:Suwa clan|Suwa clan]], and sometimes used the Suwa surname. His official titles included Shinano-no-Kami and Kii-no-Kami. Yoritsugu was the son and heir of Takato Mitsunari (Shinano-no-Kami) and married the daughter of [[:en:Suwa Yorimitsu|Suwa Yorimitsu (Aki-no-Kami)]].

In Tenbun 14 (1545), Takeda forces attacked the allied Fujisawa Yorichika and {{ill|Ōi Sadataka|ja|大井貞隆|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Sadataka|qq=|1=Ōi Sadataka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11432233}} of {{ill|Nagakubo Castle|ja|長窪城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagakubo Castle|qq=|1=Nagakubo Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11653547}}. <!--Takatoo Yoritsugu--><!--Takatoo Yoritsugu--><!--Takatoo Yoritsugu--><!--Takatoo Yoritsugu--> [[Category:Local Lords of Shinano Province]] [[Category:Sengoku Warlords]] [[Category:Takato Clan]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> <!--zhwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{wikidata link|Q11672966}}

revision.comment: Text replacement - "|qq=draft|" to "|"
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:تاکاتوئو یوریتسوگو]][[it:Takatō Yoritsugu]][[ja:高遠頼継]][[zh:高遠賴繼]] {{Infobox person|death_date=1552|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|occupation={{ill|military commander|WD=Q11545923}}|title={{ill|daimyo|WD=Q61982}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11672966]] Wikidata name: Takatō Yoritsugu [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:45, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Takato Yoritsugu''' (高遠 頼継, birth year unknown – [[:en:Tenbun|Tenbun]] 21, [[August 16 (lunar calendar)|August 16]] ([[:en:September 4|September 4]], [[:en:1552|1552]])?) was a {{ill|Samurai|ja|武士|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Samurai|1=bushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1975935}} during Japan’s [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]]. He was a local lord ([[:en:Kokujin|Kokujin]]) of [[:en:Shinano Province|Shinano Province]], serving as the lord of [[:en:Takatō Castle|Takato Castle]] (located in present-day [[:en:Ina, Nagano|Ina, Nagano]], [[:en:Takatō, Nagano|Takato Town]]). As a retainer of the {{ill|Takeda clan|ja|甲斐武田氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda clan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 武田氏}}, he held the position of {{ill|Sakikata-shū|ja|信濃先方衆|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakikata-shū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in charge of the Shinano frontlines. He belonged to the {{ill|Takato clan|ja|高遠氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takato clan|1=Takato clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11672957}}, a cadet branch of the [[:en:Suwa clan|Suwa clan]], and sometimes used the Suwa surname. His official titles included Shinano-no-Kami and Kii-no-Kami. Yoritsugu was the son and heir of Takato Mitsunari (Shinano-no-Kami) and married the daughter of [[:en:Suwa Yorimitsu|Suwa Yorimitsu (Aki-no-Kami)]].
== Biography == Yoritsugu is said to have been the son or grandson of {{ill|Takato Tsugimune|ja|高遠継宗|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takato Tsugimune|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who opposed the Suwa main family during internal conflicts. After {{ill|Suwa Yorimitsu (Aki-no-Kami)|ja|諏訪頼満|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Yorimitsu (Aki-no-Kami)|1=Suwa Yorimitsu (Aki-no-Kami)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11631959}} unified the Suwa clan, Yoritsugu initially resisted but eventually became a vassal and married Yorimitsu’s daughter.
During the Sengoku period, the Takeda clan of [[:en:Kai Province|Kai Province]] and the Suwa clan of Shinano Province had a long-standing alliance. However, in Tenbun 10 (1542), {{ill|Takeda Harunobu (Shingen)|ja|武田晴信|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Harunobu (Shingen)|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 武田信玄|1=Takeda Shingen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q276404}} (later known as Takeda Shingen) ascended as the Takeda clan head and began an aggressive campaign to conquer Shinano, breaking the alliance and invading the Suwa domain. On [[:en:July 2|July 2]], Tenbun 11 (1542), Yoritsugu assisted the Takeda in attacking {{ill|Uehara Castle|ja|上原城|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uehara Castle|1=Uehara Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11358576}}, the base of [[:en:Suwa Yorishige|Suwa Yorishige]], who surrendered to the Takeda. Yorishige was taken to {{ill|Kōfu|ja|甲府|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōfu|1=Kōfu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11577765}} and forced to commit suicide later that month. The Suwa domain was divided between the Takeda and Yoritsugu, with Yoritsugu controlling the western half.
Yoritsugu, aiming to restore the Suwa clan's authority, allied with {{ill|Fujisawa Yorichika|ja|藤沢頼親|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujisawa Yorichika|1=Fujisawa Yorichika|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11624578}} of {{ill|Fukuyo Castle|ja|福与城|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukuyo Castle|1=Fukuyo Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11591217}} in {{ill|Ina District, Nagano|ja|伊那郡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ina District, Nagano|1=Ina district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11380911}} to invade Takeda territory. However, on [[:en:September 25|September 25]], he was defeated in the {{ill|Battle of Miyagawa (1542)|ja|宮川の戦い|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Battle of Miyagawa (1542)|1=Battle of Miyagawa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17214872}} and forced to retreat from the Suwa region.
In Tenbun 14 (1545), Takeda forces attacked the allied Fujisawa Yorichika and {{ill|Ōi Sadataka|ja|大井貞隆|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Sadataka|1=Ōi Sadataka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11432233}} of {{ill|Nagakubo Castle|ja|長窪城|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagakubo Castle|1=Nagakubo Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11653547}}. Takato Castle fell to the Takeda on [[:en:April 17|April 17]], and Yoritsugu surrendered, traveling to Kōfu to submit to Takeda authority. After the castle’s capture, the Takeda fortified Takato Castle as a strategic base for controlling Shinano.
In Tenbun 17 (1548), after the Takeda’s defeat at the [[:en:Battle of Uedahara|Battle of Uedahara]] against [[:en:Murakami Yoshikiyo|Murakami Yoshikiyo]], unrest spread in Takeda-controlled territories. Yoritsugu returned to Takato Castle on [[:en:April 3|April 3]] (as noted in the *Kōhakusai-ki*).
Yoritsugu continued serving the Takeda but was reportedly forced to commit suicide during a campaign in {{ill|Shimoinadani|ja|下伊那|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimoinadani|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 南信州地域|1=Minami Shinshū area|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11666112}} in Tenbun 21 (1552). His posthumous name was 大用普徹大禅定院 (Daiyō Futtetsu Daizendō-in). Following his death, [[:en:Hoshina Masatoshi|Hoshina Masatoshi]], a vassal of the Takato clan, rose to prominence under the Takeda. While traditionally believed to mark the Takato clan's extinction, recent studies suggest that [[:en:Takeda Katsuyori|Takeda Katsuyori]], Takeda Shingen's fourth son, inherited the Takato-Suwa lineage.<ref>Marushima Kazuhiro, "Kōyasan Jōkei-in's *Kōkoku Kako-chō*: The *Past Records of Kōshū Monthly Memorials*," *Takeda Clan Studies,* No. 34, 2006.</ref>
== References == {{reflist}}
== Sources == * [[Kuroda Motoki]], "Takato Yoritsugu," *Takeda Shingen Encyclopedia* * Tanaka Toyoshige, *Shinano Medieval Samurai Biographies,* [[Shinano Mainichi Newspaper]], 2016. * [https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%AB%8F%E8%A8%AA%E9%A0%BC%E7%B6%99-85402 Kotobank: Suwa Yoritsugu] == See Also == * {{ill|Takato Tsugimune|ja|高遠継宗|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takato Tsugimune|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Takato Yorimune (Renpōken)|ja|高遠頼宗|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takato Yorimune (Renpōken)|1=Takatō Yorimune|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11672964}} ==References== {{reflist}} {{Suwa Faith}}

Yoritsugu continued serving the Takeda but was reportedly forced to commit suicide during a campaign in {{ill|Shimoinadani|ja|下伊那|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimoinadani|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 南信州地域|1=Minami Shinshū area|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11666112}} in Tenbun 21 (1552). His posthumous name was 大用普徹大禅定院 (Daiyō Futtetsu Daizendō-in). Following his death, [[:en:Hoshina Masatoshi|Hoshina Masatoshi]], a vassal of the Takato clan, rose to prominence under the Takeda. While traditionally believed to mark the Takato clan's extinction, recent studies suggest that [[:en:Takeda Katsuyori|Takeda Katsuyori]], Takeda Shingen's fourth son, inherited the Takato-Suwa lineage.<ref>Marushima Kazuhiro, "Kōyasan Jōkei-in's *Kōkoku Kako-chō*: The *Past Records of Kōshū Monthly Memorials*," *Takeda Clan Studies,* No. 34, 2006.</ref>

<!--Takatoo Yoritsugu--><!--Takatoo Yoritsugu--><!--Takatoo Yoritsugu--><!--Takatoo Yoritsugu--> [[Category:Local Lords of Shinano Province]] [[Category:Sengoku Warlords]] [[Category:Takato Clan]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> <!--zhwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{wikidata link|Q11672966}} [[Category:Wikidata no short description]]

revision.comment: Bot: Add Wikidata has English label - based on Wikidata English label
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:تاکاتوئو یوریتسوگو]][[it:Takatō Yoritsugu]][[ja:高遠頼継]][[zh:高遠賴繼]] {{Infobox person|death_date=1552|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|occupation={{ill|military commander|WD=Q11545923}}|title={{ill|daimyo|WD=Q61982}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11672966]] Wikidata name: Takatō Yoritsugu [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:45, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Takato Yoritsugu''' (高遠 頼継, birth year unknown – [[:en:Tenbun|Tenbun]] 21, [[August 16 (lunar calendar)|August 16]] ([[:en:September 4|September 4]], [[:en:1552|1552]])?) was a {{ill|Samurai|ja|武士|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Samurai|1=bushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1975935}} during Japan’s [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]]. He was a local lord ([[:en:Kokujin|Kokujin]]) of [[:en:Shinano Province|Shinano Province]], serving as the lord of [[:en:Takatō Castle|Takato Castle]] (located in present-day [[:en:Ina, Nagano|Ina, Nagano]], [[:en:Takatō, Nagano|Takato Town]]). As a retainer of the {{ill|Takeda clan|ja|甲斐武田氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda clan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 武田氏}}, he held the position of {{ill|Sakikata-shū|ja|信濃先方衆|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakikata-shū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in charge of the Shinano frontlines. He belonged to the {{ill|Takato clan|ja|高遠氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takato clan|1=Takato clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11672957}}, a cadet branch of the [[:en:Suwa clan|Suwa clan]], and sometimes used the Suwa surname. His official titles included Shinano-no-Kami and Kii-no-Kami. Yoritsugu was the son and heir of Takato Mitsunari (Shinano-no-Kami) and married the daughter of [[:en:Suwa Yorimitsu|Suwa Yorimitsu (Aki-no-Kami)]].
== Biography == Yoritsugu is said to have been the son or grandson of {{ill|Takato Tsugimune|ja|高遠継宗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takato Tsugimune|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who opposed the Suwa main family during internal conflicts. After {{ill|Suwa Yorimitsu (Aki-no-Kami)|ja|諏訪頼満|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Yorimitsu (Aki-no-Kami)|1=Suwa Yorimitsu (Aki-no-Kami)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11631959}} unified the Suwa clan, Yoritsugu initially resisted but eventually became a vassal and married Yorimitsu’s daughter.
During the Sengoku period, the Takeda clan of [[:en:Kai Province|Kai Province]] and the Suwa clan of Shinano Province had a long-standing alliance. However, in Tenbun 10 (1542), {{ill|Takeda Harunobu (Shingen)|ja|武田晴信|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Harunobu (Shingen)|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 武田信玄|1=Takeda Shingen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q276404}} (later known as Takeda Shingen) ascended as the Takeda clan head and began an aggressive campaign to conquer Shinano, breaking the alliance and invading the Suwa domain. On [[:en:July 2|July 2]], Tenbun 11 (1542), Yoritsugu assisted the Takeda in attacking {{ill|Uehara Castle|ja|上原城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uehara Castle|1=Uehara Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11358576}}, the base of [[:en:Suwa Yorishige|Suwa Yorishige]], who surrendered to the Takeda. Yorishige was taken to {{ill|Kōfu|ja|甲府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōfu|1=Kōfu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11577765}} and forced to commit suicide later that month. The Suwa domain was divided between the Takeda and Yoritsugu, with Yoritsugu controlling the western half.
Yoritsugu, aiming to restore the Suwa clan's authority, allied with {{ill|Fujisawa Yorichika|ja|藤沢頼親|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujisawa Yorichika|1=Fujisawa Yorichika|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11624578}} of {{ill|Fukuyo Castle|ja|福与城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukuyo Castle|1=Fukuyo Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11591217}} in {{ill|Ina District, Nagano|ja|伊那郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ina District, Nagano|1=Ina district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11380911}} to invade Takeda territory. However, on [[:en:September 25|September 25]], he was defeated in the {{ill|Battle of Miyagawa (1542)|ja|宮川の戦い|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Battle of Miyagawa (1542)|1=Battle of Miyagawa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17214872}} and forced to retreat from the Suwa region.
In Tenbun 14 (1545), Takeda forces attacked the allied Fujisawa Yorichika and {{ill|Ōi Sadataka|ja|大井貞隆|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Sadataka|1=Ōi Sadataka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11432233}} of {{ill|Nagakubo Castle|ja|長窪城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagakubo Castle|1=Nagakubo Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11653547}}. Takato Castle fell to the Takeda on [[:en:April 17|April 17]], and Yoritsugu surrendered, traveling to Kōfu to submit to Takeda authority. After the castle’s capture, the Takeda fortified Takato Castle as a strategic base for controlling Shinano.
In Tenbun 17 (1548), after the Takeda’s defeat at the [[:en:Battle of Uedahara|Battle of Uedahara]] against [[:en:Murakami Yoshikiyo|Murakami Yoshikiyo]], unrest spread in Takeda-controlled territories. Yoritsugu returned to Takato Castle on [[:en:April 3|April 3]] (as noted in the *Kōhakusai-ki*).
Yoritsugu continued serving the Takeda but was reportedly forced to commit suicide during a campaign in {{ill|Shimoinadani|ja|下伊那|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimoinadani|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 南信州地域|1=Minami Shinshū area|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11666112}} in Tenbun 21 (1552). His posthumous name was 大用普徹大禅定院 (Daiyō Futtetsu Daizendō-in). Following his death, [[:en:Hoshina Masatoshi|Hoshina Masatoshi]], a vassal of the Takato clan, rose to prominence under the Takeda. While traditionally believed to mark the Takato clan's extinction, recent studies suggest that [[:en:Takeda Katsuyori|Takeda Katsuyori]], Takeda Shingen's fourth son, inherited the Takato-Suwa lineage.<ref>Marushima Kazuhiro, "Kōyasan Jōkei-in's *Kōkoku Kako-chō*: The *Past Records of Kōshū Monthly Memorials*," *Takeda Clan Studies,* No. 34, 2006.</ref>

Yorishige was taken to {{ill|Kōfu|ja|甲府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōfu|1=Kōfu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11577765}} and forced to commit suicide later that month. The Suwa domain was divided between the Takeda and Yoritsugu, with Yoritsugu controlling the western half.
Yoritsugu, aiming to restore the Suwa clan's authority, allied with {{ill|Fujisawa Yorichika|ja|藤沢頼親|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujisawa Yorichika|1=Fujisawa Yorichika|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11624578}} of {{ill|Fukuyo Castle|ja|福与城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukuyo Castle|1=Fukuyo Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11591217}} in {{ill|Ina District, Nagano|ja|伊那郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ina District, Nagano|1=Ina district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11380911}} to invade Takeda territory. However, on [[:en:September 25|September 25]], he was defeated in the {{ill|Battle of Miyagawa (1542)|ja|宮川の戦い|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Battle of Miyagawa (1542)|1=Battle of Miyagawa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17214872}} and forced to retreat from the Suwa region.
In Tenbun 14 (1545), Takeda forces attacked the allied Fujisawa Yorichika and {{ill|Ōi Sadataka|ja|大井貞隆|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Sadataka|1=Ōi Sadataka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11432233}} of {{ill|Nagakubo Castle|ja|長窪城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagakubo Castle|1=Nagakubo Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11653547}}. Takato Castle fell to the Takeda on [[:en:April 17|April 17]], and Yoritsugu surrendered, traveling to Kōfu to submit to Takeda authority. After the castle’s capture, the Takeda fortified Takato Castle as a strategic base for controlling Shinano.
In Tenbun 17 (1548), after the Takeda’s defeat at the [[:en:Battle of Uedahara|Battle of Uedahara]] against [[:en:Murakami Yoshikiyo|Murakami Yoshikiyo]], unrest spread in Takeda-controlled territories. Yoritsugu returned to Takato Castle on [[:en:April 3|April 3]] (as noted in the *Kōhakusai-ki*).
Yoritsugu continued serving the Takeda but was reportedly forced to commit suicide during a campaign in {{ill|Shimoinadani|ja|下伊那|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimoinadani|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 南信州地域|1=Minami Shinshū area|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11666112}} in Tenbun 21 (1552). His posthumous name was 大用普徹大禅定院 (Daiyō Futtetsu Daizendō-in). Following his death, [[:en:Hoshina Masatoshi|Hoshina Masatoshi]], a vassal of the Takato clan, rose to prominence under the Takeda. While traditionally believed to mark the Takato clan's extinction, recent studies suggest that [[:en:Takeda Katsuyori|Takeda Katsuyori]], Takeda Shingen's fourth son, inherited the Takato-Suwa lineage.<ref>Marushima Kazuhiro, "Kōyasan Jōkei-in's *Kōkoku Kako-chō*: The *Past Records of Kōshū Monthly Memorials*," *Takeda Clan Studies,* No. 34, 2006.</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:تاکاتوئو یوریتسوگو]][[it:Takatō Yoritsugu]][[ja:高遠頼継]][[zh:高遠賴繼]] {{Infobox person|death_date=1552|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|occupation={{ill|military commander|WD=Q11545923}}|title={{ill|daimyo|WD=Q61982}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11672966]] Wikidata name: Takatō Yoritsugu [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:45, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Takato Yoritsugu''' (高遠 頼継, birth year unknown – [[:en:Tenbun|Tenbun]] 21, [[August 16 (lunar calendar)|August 16]] ([[:en:September 4|September 4]], [[:en:1552|1552]])?) was a {{ill|Samurai|ja|武士|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Samurai|1=bushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1975935}} during Japan’s [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]]. He was a local lord ([[:en:Kokujin|Kokujin]]) of [[:en:Shinano Province|Shinano Province]], serving as the lord of [[:en:Takatō Castle|Takato Castle]] (located in present-day [[:en:Ina, Nagano|Ina, Nagano]], [[:en:Takatō, Nagano|Takato Town]]). As a retainer of the {{ill|Takeda clan|ja|甲斐武田氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda clan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 武田氏}}, he held the position of {{ill|Sakikata-shū|ja|信濃先方衆|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakikata-shū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in charge of the Shinano frontlines. He belonged to the {{ill|Takato clan|ja|高遠氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takato clan|1=Takato clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11672957}}, a cadet branch of the [[:en:Suwa clan|Suwa clan]], and sometimes used the Suwa surname. His official titles included Shinano-no-Kami and Kii-no-Kami. Yoritsugu was the son and heir of Takato Mitsunari (Shinano-no-Kami) and married the daughter of [[:en:Suwa Yorimitsu|Suwa Yorimitsu (Aki-no-Kami)]].
== Biography == Yoritsugu is said to have been the son or grandson of {{ill|Takato Tsugimune|ja|高遠継宗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takato Tsugimune|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who opposed the Suwa main family during internal conflicts. After {{ill|Suwa Yorimitsu (Aki-no-Kami)|ja|諏訪頼満|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Yorimitsu (Aki-no-Kami)|1=Suwa Yorimitsu (Aki-no-Kami)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11631959}} unified the Suwa clan, Yoritsugu initially resisted but eventually became a vassal and married Yorimitsu’s daughter.
During the Sengoku period, the Takeda clan of [[:en:Kai Province|Kai Province]] and the Suwa clan of Shinano Province had a long-standing alliance. However, in Tenbun 10 (1542), {{ill|Takeda Harunobu (Shingen)|ja|武田晴信|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Harunobu (Shingen)|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 武田信玄|1=Takeda Shingen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q276404}} (later known as Takeda Shingen) ascended as the Takeda clan head and began an aggressive campaign to conquer Shinano, breaking the alliance and invading the Suwa domain. On [[:en:July 2|July 2]], Tenbun 11 (1542), Yoritsugu assisted the Takeda in attacking {{ill|Uehara Castle|ja|上原城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uehara Castle|1=Uehara Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11358576}}, the base of [[:en:Suwa Yorishige|Suwa Yorishige]], who surrendered to the Takeda. Yorishige was taken to {{ill|Kōfu|ja|甲府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōfu|1=Kōfu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11577765}} and forced to commit suicide later that month. The Suwa domain was divided between the Takeda and Yoritsugu, with Yoritsugu controlling the western half.
Yoritsugu, aiming to restore the Suwa clan's authority, allied with {{ill|Fujisawa Yorichika|ja|藤沢頼親|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujisawa Yorichika|1=Fujisawa Yorichika|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11624578}} of {{ill|Fukuyo Castle|ja|福与城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukuyo Castle|1=Fukuyo Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11591217}} in {{ill|Ina District, Nagano|ja|伊那郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ina District, Nagano|1=Ina district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11380911}} to invade Takeda territory. However, on [[:en:September 25|September 25]], he was defeated in the {{ill|Battle of Miyagawa (1542)|ja|宮川の戦い|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Battle of Miyagawa (1542)|1=Battle of Miyagawa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17214872}} and forced to retreat from the Suwa region.
In Tenbun 14 (1545), Takeda forces attacked the allied Fujisawa Yorichika and {{ill|Ōi Sadataka|ja|大井貞隆|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Sadataka|1=Ōi Sadataka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11432233}} of {{ill|Nagakubo Castle|ja|長窪城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagakubo Castle|1=Nagakubo Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11653547}}. Takato Castle fell to the Takeda on [[:en:April 17|April 17]], and Yoritsugu surrendered, traveling to Kōfu to submit to Takeda authority. After the castle’s capture, the Takeda fortified Takato Castle as a strategic base for controlling Shinano.
In Tenbun 17 (1548), after the Takeda’s defeat at the [[:en:Battle of Uedahara|Battle of Uedahara]] against [[:en:Murakami Yoshikiyo|Murakami Yoshikiyo]], unrest spread in Takeda-controlled territories. Yoritsugu returned to Takato Castle on [[:en:April 3|April 3]] (as noted in the *Kōhakusai-ki*).
Yoritsugu continued serving the Takeda but was reportedly forced to commit suicide during a campaign in {{ill|Shimoinadani|ja|下伊那|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimoinadani|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 南信州地域|1=Minami Shinshū area|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11666112}} in Tenbun 21 (1552). His posthumous name was 大用普徹大禅定院 (Daiyō Futtetsu Daizendō-in). Following his death, [[:en:Hoshina Masatoshi|Hoshina Masatoshi]], a vassal of the Takato clan, rose to prominence under the Takeda. While traditionally believed to mark the Takato clan's extinction, recent studies suggest that [[:en:Takeda Katsuyori|Takeda Katsuyori]], Takeda Shingen's fourth son, inherited the Takato-Suwa lineage.<ref>Marushima Kazuhiro, "Kōyasan Jōkei-in's *Kōkoku Kako-chō*: The *Past Records of Kōshū Monthly Memorials*," *Takeda Clan Studies,* No. 34, 2006.</ref>

As a retainer of the {{ill|Takeda clan|ja|甲斐武田氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda clan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 武田氏}}, he held the position of {{ill|Sakikata-shū|ja|信濃先方衆|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakikata-shū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in charge of the Shinano frontlines. He belonged to the {{ill|Takato clan|ja|高遠氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takato clan|1=Takato clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11672957}}, a cadet branch of the [[:en:Suwa clan|Suwa clan]], and sometimes used the Suwa surname. His official titles included Shinano-no-Kami and Kii-no-Kami. Yoritsugu was the son and heir of Takato Mitsunari (Shinano-no-Kami) and married the daughter of [[:en:Suwa Yorimitsu|Suwa Yorimitsu (Aki-no-Kami)]].
== Biography == Yoritsugu is said to have been the son or grandson of {{ill|Takato Tsugimune|ja|高遠継宗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takato Tsugimune|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who opposed the Suwa main family during internal conflicts. After {{ill|Suwa Yorimitsu (Aki-no-Kami)|ja|諏訪頼満|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Yorimitsu (Aki-no-Kami)|1=Suwa Yorimitsu (Aki-no-Kami)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11631959}} unified the Suwa clan, Yoritsugu initially resisted but eventually became a vassal and married Yorimitsu’s daughter.
During the Sengoku period, the Takeda clan of [[:en:Kai Province|Kai Province]] and the Suwa clan of Shinano Province had a long-standing alliance. However, in Tenbun 10 (1542), {{ill|Takeda Harunobu (Shingen)|ja|武田晴信|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Harunobu (Shingen)|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 武田信玄|1=Takeda Shingen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q276404}} (later known as Takeda Shingen) ascended as the Takeda clan head and began an aggressive campaign to conquer Shinano, breaking the alliance and invading the Suwa domain. On [[:en:July 2|July 2]], Tenbun 11 (1542), Yoritsugu assisted the Takeda in attacking {{ill|Uehara Castle|ja|上原城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uehara Castle|1=Uehara Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11358576}}, the base of [[:en:Suwa Yorishige|Suwa Yorishige]], who surrendered to the Takeda. Yorishige was taken to {{ill|Kōfu|ja|甲府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōfu|1=Kōfu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11577765}} and forced to commit suicide later that month. The Suwa domain was divided between the Takeda and Yoritsugu, with Yoritsugu controlling the western half.
Yoritsugu, aiming to restore the Suwa clan's authority, allied with {{ill|Fujisawa Yorichika|ja|藤沢頼親|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujisawa Yorichika|1=Fujisawa Yorichika|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11624578}} of {{ill|Fukuyo Castle|ja|福与城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukuyo Castle|1=Fukuyo Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11591217}} in {{ill|Ina District, Nagano|ja|伊那郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ina District, Nagano|1=Ina district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11380911}} to invade Takeda territory. However, on [[:en:September 25|September 25]], he was defeated in the {{ill|Battle of Miyagawa (1542)|ja|宮川の戦い|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Battle of Miyagawa (1542)|1=Battle of Miyagawa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17214872}} and forced to retreat from the Suwa region.
In Tenbun 14 (1545), Takeda forces attacked the allied Fujisawa Yorichika and {{ill|Ōi Sadataka|ja|大井貞隆|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Sadataka|1=Ōi Sadataka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11432233}} of {{ill|Nagakubo Castle|ja|長窪城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagakubo Castle|1=Nagakubo Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11653547}}. Takato Castle fell to the Takeda on [[:en:April 17|April 17]], and Yoritsugu surrendered, traveling to Kōfu to submit to Takeda authority. After the castle’s capture, the Takeda fortified Takato Castle as a strategic base for controlling Shinano.
In Tenbun 17 (1548), after the Takeda’s defeat at the [[:en:Battle of Uedahara|Battle of Uedahara]] against [[:en:Murakami Yoshikiyo|Murakami Yoshikiyo]], unrest spread in Takeda-controlled territories. Yoritsugu returned to Takato Castle on [[:en:April 3|April 3]] (as noted in the *Kōhakusai-ki*).
Yoritsugu continued serving the Takeda but was reportedly forced to commit suicide during a campaign in {{ill|Shimoinadani|ja|下伊那|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimoinadani|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 南信州地域|1=Minami Shinshū area|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11666112}} in Tenbun 21 (1552). His posthumous name was 大用普徹大禅定院 (Daiyō Futtetsu Daizendō-in). Following his death, [[:en:Hoshina Masatoshi|Hoshina Masatoshi]], a vassal of the Takato clan, rose to prominence under the Takeda. While traditionally believed to mark the Takato clan's extinction, recent studies suggest that [[:en:Takeda Katsuyori|Takeda Katsuyori]], Takeda Shingen's fourth son, inherited the Takato-Suwa lineage.<ref>Marushima Kazuhiro, "Kōyasan Jōkei-in's *Kōkoku Kako-chō*: The *Past Records of Kōshū Monthly Memorials*," *Takeda Clan Studies,* No. 34, 2006.</ref>

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:تاکاتوئو یوریتسوگو]][[it:Takatō Yoritsugu]][[ja:高遠頼継]][[zh:高遠賴繼]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:تاکاتوئو یوریتسوگو]][[it:Takatō Yoritsugu]][[ja:高遠頼継]][[zh:高遠賴繼]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:تاکاتوئو یوریتسوگو]][[it:Takatō Yoritsugu]][[ja:高遠頼継]][[zh:高遠賴繼]] {{Infobox person|death_date=1552|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|occupation={{ill|military commander|WD=Q11545923}}|title={{ill|daimyo|WD=Q61982}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11672966]] Wikidata name: Takatō Yoritsugu [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:45, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Takato Yoritsugu''' (高遠 頼継, birth year unknown – [[:en:Tenbun|Tenbun]] 21, [[August 16 (lunar calendar)|August 16]] ([[:en:September 4|September 4]], [[:en:1552|1552]])?) was a {{ill|Samurai|ja|武士|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Samurai|1=bushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1975935}} during Japan’s [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]]. He was a local lord ([[:en:Kokujin|Kokujin]]) of [[:en:Shinano Province|Shinano Province]], serving as the lord of [[:en:Takatō Castle|Takato Castle]] (located in present-day [[:en:Ina, Nagano|Ina, Nagano]], [[:en:Takatō, Nagano|Takato Town]]). As a retainer of the {{ill|Takeda clan|ja|甲斐武田氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda clan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 武田氏}}, he held the position of {{ill|Sakikata-shū|ja|信濃先方衆|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakikata-shū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in charge of the Shinano frontlines. He belonged to the {{ill|Takato clan|ja|高遠氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takato clan|1=Takato clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11672957}}, a cadet branch of the [[:en:Suwa clan|Suwa clan]], and sometimes used the Suwa surname. His official titles included Shinano-no-Kami and Kii-no-Kami. Yoritsugu was the son and heir of Takato Mitsunari (Shinano-no-Kami) and married the daughter of [[:en:Suwa Yorimitsu|Suwa Yorimitsu (Aki-no-Kami)]].
== Biography == Yoritsugu is said to have been the son or grandson of {{ill|Takato Tsugimune|ja|高遠継宗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takato Tsugimune|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who opposed the Suwa main family during internal conflicts. After {{ill|Suwa Yorimitsu (Aki-no-Kami)|ja|諏訪頼満|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Yorimitsu (Aki-no-Kami)|1=Suwa Yorimitsu (Aki-no-Kami)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11631959}} unified the Suwa clan, Yoritsugu initially resisted but eventually became a vassal and married Yorimitsu’s daughter.
During the Sengoku period, the Takeda clan of [[:en:Kai Province|Kai Province]] and the Suwa clan of Shinano Province had a long-standing alliance. However, in Tenbun 10 (1542), {{ill|Takeda Harunobu (Shingen)|ja|武田晴信|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Harunobu (Shingen)|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 武田信玄|1=Takeda Shingen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q276404}} (later known as Takeda Shingen) ascended as the Takeda clan head and began an aggressive campaign to conquer Shinano, breaking the alliance and invading the Suwa domain. On [[:en:July 2|July 2]], Tenbun 11 (1542), Yoritsugu assisted the Takeda in attacking {{ill|Uehara Castle|ja|上原城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uehara Castle|1=Uehara Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11358576}}, the base of [[:en:Suwa Yorishige|Suwa Yorishige]], who surrendered to the Takeda. Yorishige was taken to {{ill|Kōfu|ja|甲府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōfu|1=Kōfu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11577765}} and forced to commit suicide later that month. The Suwa domain was divided between the Takeda and Yoritsugu, with Yoritsugu controlling the western half.
Yoritsugu, aiming to restore the Suwa clan's authority, allied with {{ill|Fujisawa Yorichika|ja|藤沢頼親|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujisawa Yorichika|1=Fujisawa Yorichika|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11624578}} of {{ill|Fukuyo Castle|ja|福与城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukuyo Castle|1=Fukuyo Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11591217}} in {{ill|Ina District, Nagano|ja|伊那郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ina District, Nagano|1=Ina district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11380911}} to invade Takeda territory. However, on [[:en:September 25|September 25]], he was defeated in the {{ill|Battle of Miyagawa (1542)|ja|宮川の戦い|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Battle of Miyagawa (1542)|1=Battle of Miyagawa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17214872}} and forced to retreat from the Suwa region.
In Tenbun 14 (1545), Takeda forces attacked the allied Fujisawa Yorichika and {{ill|Ōi Sadataka|ja|大井貞隆|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Sadataka|1=Ōi Sadataka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11432233}} of {{ill|Nagakubo Castle|ja|長窪城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagakubo Castle|1=Nagakubo Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11653547}}. Takato Castle fell to the Takeda on [[:en:April 17|April 17]], and Yoritsugu surrendered, traveling to Kōfu to submit to Takeda authority. After the castle’s capture, the Takeda fortified Takato Castle as a strategic base for controlling Shinano.
In Tenbun 17 (1548), after the Takeda’s defeat at the [[:en:Battle of Uedahara|Battle of Uedahara]] against [[:en:Murakami Yoshikiyo|Murakami Yoshikiyo]], unrest spread in Takeda-controlled territories. Yoritsugu returned to Takato Castle on [[:en:April 3|April 3]] (as noted in the *Kōhakusai-ki*).
Yoritsugu continued serving the Takeda but was reportedly forced to commit suicide during a campaign in {{ill|Shimoinadani|ja|下伊那|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimoinadani|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 南信州地域|1=Minami Shinshū area|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11666112}} in Tenbun 21 (1552). His posthumous name was 大用普徹大禅定院 (Daiyō Futtetsu Daizendō-in). Following his death, [[:en:Hoshina Masatoshi|Hoshina Masatoshi]], a vassal of the Takato clan, rose to prominence under the Takeda. While traditionally believed to mark the Takato clan's extinction, recent studies suggest that [[:en:Takeda Katsuyori|Takeda Katsuyori]], Takeda Shingen's fourth son, inherited the Takato-Suwa lineage.<ref>Marushima Kazuhiro, "Kōyasan Jōkei-in's *Kōkoku Kako-chō*: The *Past Records of Kōshū Monthly Memorials*," *Takeda Clan Studies,* No. 34, 2006.</ref>
== References == {{reflist}}
== Sources == * [[Kuroda Motoki]], "Takato Yoritsugu," *Takeda Shingen Encyclopedia* * Tanaka Toyoshige, *Shinano Medieval Samurai Biographies,* [[Shinano Mainichi Newspaper]], 2016. * [https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%AB%8F%E8%A8%AA%E9%A0%BC%E7%B6%99-85402 Kotobank: Suwa Yoritsugu] == See Also == * {{ill|Takato Tsugimune|ja|高遠継宗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takato Tsugimune|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Takato Yorimune (Renpōken)|ja|高遠頼宗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takato Yorimune (Renpōken)|1=Takatō Yorimune|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11672964}} ==References== {{reflist}} {{Suwa Faith}}

In Tenbun 14 (1545), Takeda forces attacked the allied Fujisawa Yorichika and {{ill|Ōi Sadataka|ja|大井貞隆|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Sadataka|1=Ōi Sadataka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11432233}} of {{ill|Nagakubo Castle|ja|長窪城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagakubo Castle|1=Nagakubo Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11653547}}. Takato Castle fell to the Takeda on [[:en:April 17|April 17]], and Yoritsugu surrendered, traveling to Kōfu to submit to Takeda authority. After the castle’s capture, the Takeda fortified Takato Castle as a strategic base for controlling Shinano.
In Tenbun 17 (1548), after the Takeda’s defeat at the [[:en:Battle of Uedahara|Battle of Uedahara]] against [[:en:Murakami Yoshikiyo|Murakami Yoshikiyo]], unrest spread in Takeda-controlled territories. Yoritsugu returned to Takato Castle on [[:en:April 3|April 3]] (as noted in the *Kōhakusai-ki*).
Yoritsugu continued serving the Takeda but was reportedly forced to commit suicide during a campaign in {{ill|Shimoinadani|ja|下伊那|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimoinadani|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 南信州地域|1=Minami Shinshū area|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11666112}} in Tenbun 21 (1552). His posthumous name was 大用普徹大禅定院 (Daiyō Futtetsu Daizendō-in). Following his death, [[:en:Hoshina Masatoshi|Hoshina Masatoshi]], a vassal of the Takato clan, rose to prominence under the Takeda. While traditionally believed to mark the Takato clan's extinction, recent studies suggest that [[:en:Takeda Katsuyori|Takeda Katsuyori]], Takeda Shingen's fourth son, inherited the Takato-Suwa lineage.<ref>Marushima Kazuhiro, "Kōyasan Jōkei-in's *Kōkoku Kako-chō*: The *Past Records of Kōshū Monthly Memorials*," *Takeda Clan Studies,* No. 34, 2006.</ref>
== References == {{reflist}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:تاکاتوئو یوریتسوگو]][[it:Takatō Yoritsugu]][[ja:高遠頼継]][[zh:高遠賴繼]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:تاکاتوئو یوریتسوگو]][[it:Takatō Yoritsugu]][[ja:高遠頼継]][[zh:高遠賴繼]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:تاکاتوئو یوریتسوگو]][[it:Takatō Yoritsugu]][[ja:高遠頼継]][[zh:高遠賴繼]] {{Infobox person|death_date=1552|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|occupation={{ill|military commander|WD=Q11545923}}|title={{ill|daimyo|WD=Q61982}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11672966]] Wikidata name: Takatō Yoritsugu [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:45, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Takato Yoritsugu''' (高遠 頼継, birth year unknown – [[:en:Tenbun|Tenbun]] 21, [[August 16 (lunar calendar)|August 16]] ([[:en:September 4|September 4]], [[:en:1552|1552]])?) was a {{ill|Samurai|ja|武士|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Samurai|1=bushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1975935}} during Japan’s [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]]. He was a local lord ([[:en:Kokujin|Kokujin]]) of [[:en:Shinano Province|Shinano Province]], serving as the lord of [[:en:Takatō Castle|Takato Castle]] (located in present-day [[:en:Ina, Nagano|Ina, Nagano]], [[:en:Takatō, Nagano|Takato Town]]). As a retainer of the {{ill|Takeda clan|ja|甲斐武田氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda clan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 武田氏}}, he held the position of {{ill|Sakikata-shū|ja|信濃先方衆|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakikata-shū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in charge of the Shinano frontlines. He belonged to the {{ill|Takato clan|ja|高遠氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takato clan|1=Takato clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11672957}}, a cadet branch of the [[:en:Suwa clan|Suwa clan]], and sometimes used the Suwa surname. His official titles included Shinano-no-Kami and Kii-no-Kami. Yoritsugu was the son and heir of Takato Mitsunari (Shinano-no-Kami) and married the daughter of [[:en:Suwa Yorimitsu|Suwa Yorimitsu (Aki-no-Kami)]].
== Biography == Yoritsugu is said to have been the son or grandson of {{ill|Takato Tsugimune|ja|高遠継宗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takato Tsugimune|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who opposed the Suwa main family during internal conflicts. After {{ill|Suwa Yorimitsu (Aki-no-Kami)|ja|諏訪頼満|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Yorimitsu (Aki-no-Kami)|1=Suwa Yorimitsu (Aki-no-Kami)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11631959}} unified the Suwa clan, Yoritsugu initially resisted but eventually became a vassal and married Yorimitsu’s daughter.
During the Sengoku period, the Takeda clan of [[:en:Kai Province|Kai Province]] and the Suwa clan of Shinano Province had a long-standing alliance. However, in Tenbun 10 (1542), {{ill|Takeda Harunobu (Shingen)|ja|武田晴信|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Harunobu (Shingen)|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 武田信玄|1=Takeda Shingen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q276404}} (later known as Takeda Shingen) ascended as the Takeda clan head and began an aggressive campaign to conquer Shinano, breaking the alliance and invading the Suwa domain. On [[:en:July 2|July 2]], Tenbun 11 (1542), Yoritsugu assisted the Takeda in attacking {{ill|Uehara Castle|ja|上原城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uehara Castle|1=Uehara Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11358576}}, the base of [[:en:Suwa Yorishige|Suwa Yorishige]], who surrendered to the Takeda. Yorishige was taken to {{ill|Kōfu|ja|甲府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōfu|1=Kōfu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11577765}} and forced to commit suicide later that month. The Suwa domain was divided between the Takeda and Yoritsugu, with Yoritsugu controlling the western half.
Yoritsugu, aiming to restore the Suwa clan's authority, allied with {{ill|Fujisawa Yorichika|ja|藤沢頼親|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujisawa Yorichika|1=Fujisawa Yorichika|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11624578}} of {{ill|Fukuyo Castle|ja|福与城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukuyo Castle|1=Fukuyo Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11591217}} in {{ill|Ina District, Nagano|ja|伊那郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ina District, Nagano|1=Ina district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11380911}} to invade Takeda territory. However, on [[:en:September 25|September 25]], he was defeated in the {{ill|Battle of Miyagawa (1542)|ja|宮川の戦い|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Battle of Miyagawa (1542)|1=Battle of Miyagawa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17214872}} and forced to retreat from the Suwa region.
In Tenbun 14 (1545), Takeda forces attacked the allied Fujisawa Yorichika and {{ill|Ōi Sadataka|ja|大井貞隆|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Sadataka|1=Ōi Sadataka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11432233}} of {{ill|Nagakubo Castle|ja|長窪城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagakubo Castle|1=Nagakubo Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11653547}}. Takato Castle fell to the Takeda on [[:en:April 17|April 17]], and Yoritsugu surrendered, traveling to Kōfu to submit to Takeda authority. After the castle’s capture, the Takeda fortified Takato Castle as a strategic base for controlling Shinano.
In Tenbun 17 (1548), after the Takeda’s defeat at the [[:en:Battle of Uedahara|Battle of Uedahara]] against [[:en:Murakami Yoshikiyo|Murakami Yoshikiyo]], unrest spread in Takeda-controlled territories. Yoritsugu returned to Takato Castle on [[:en:April 3|April 3]] (as noted in the *Kōhakusai-ki*).
Yoritsugu continued serving the Takeda but was reportedly forced to commit suicide during a campaign in {{ill|Shimoinadani|ja|下伊那|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimoinadani|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 南信州地域|1=Minami Shinshū area|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11666112}} in Tenbun 21 (1552). His posthumous name was 大用普徹大禅定院 (Daiyō Futtetsu Daizendō-in). Following his death, [[:en:Hoshina Masatoshi|Hoshina Masatoshi]], a vassal of the Takato clan, rose to prominence under the Takeda. While traditionally believed to mark the Takato clan's extinction, recent studies suggest that [[:en:Takeda Katsuyori|Takeda Katsuyori]], Takeda Shingen's fourth son, inherited the Takato-Suwa lineage.<ref>Marushima Kazuhiro, "Kōyasan Jōkei-in's *Kōkoku Kako-chō*: The *Past Records of Kōshū Monthly Memorials*," *Takeda Clan Studies,* No. 34, 2006.</ref>

However, in Tenbun 10 (1542), {{ill|Takeda Harunobu (Shingen)|ja|武田晴信|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Harunobu (Shingen)|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 武田信玄|1=Takeda Shingen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q276404}} (later known as Takeda Shingen) ascended as the Takeda clan head and began an aggressive campaign to conquer Shinano, breaking the alliance and invading the Suwa domain. In Tenbun 17 (1548), after the Takeda’s defeat at the {{ill|Battle of Uedahara|en|Battle of Uedahara|WD=Q1358091|lt=Battle of Uedahara}} against {{ill|Murakami Yoshikiyo|en|Murakami Yoshikiyo|WD=Q1048622|lt=Murakami Yoshikiyo}}, unrest spread in Takeda-controlled territories. Following his death, {{ill|Hoshina Masatoshi|en|Hoshina Masatoshi|WD=Q2040645|lt=Hoshina Masatoshi}}, a vassal of the Takato clan, rose to prominence under the Takeda. <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:تاکاتوئو یوریتسوگو]][[it:Takatō Yoritsugu]][[ja:高遠頼継]][[zh:高遠賴繼]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:تاکاتوئو یوریتسوگو]][[it:Takatō Yoritsugu]][[ja:高遠頼継]][[zh:高遠賴繼]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:تاکاتوئو یوریتسوگو]][[it:Takatō Yoritsugu]][[ja:高遠頼継]][[zh:高遠賴繼]] {{Infobox person|death_date=1552|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|occupation={{ill|military commander|WD=Q11545923}}|title={{ill|daimyo|WD=Q61982}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11672966]] Wikidata name: Takatō Yoritsugu [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:45, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Takato Yoritsugu''' (高遠 頼継, birth year unknown – {{ill|Tenbun|en|Tenbun|WD=Q1192853|lt=Tenbun}} 21, [[August 16 (lunar calendar)|August 16]] ({{ill|September 4|en|September 4|WD=Q2855|lt=September 4}}, {{ill|1552|en|1552|WD=Q6565|lt=1552}})?) was a {{ill|Samurai|ja|武士|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Samurai|1=bushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1975935}} during Japan’s {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period}}. He was a local lord ([[:en:Kokujin|Kokujin]]) of {{ill|Shinano Province|en|Shinano Province|WD=Q289250|lt=Shinano Province}}, serving as the lord of {{ill|Takatō Castle|en|Takatō Castle|WD=Q11672954|lt=Takato Castle}} (located in present-day {{ill|Ina, Nagano|en|Ina, Nagano|WD=Q840888|lt=Ina, Nagano}}, {{ill|Takatō, Nagano|en|Takatō, Nagano|WD=Q7677451|lt=Takato Town}}). As a retainer of the {{ill|Takeda clan|ja|甲斐武田氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda clan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 武田氏}}, he held the position of {{ill|Sakikata-shū|ja|信濃先方衆|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakikata-shū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in charge of the Shinano frontlines. He belonged to the {{ill|Takato clan|ja|高遠氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takato clan|1=Takato clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11672957}}, a cadet branch of the {{ill|Suwa clan|en|Suwa clan|WD=Q2975376|lt=Suwa clan}}, and sometimes used the Suwa surname. His official titles included Shinano-no-Kami and Kii-no-Kami. Yoritsugu was the son and heir of Takato Mitsunari (Shinano-no-Kami) and married the daughter of {{ill|Suwa Yorimitsu|en|Suwa Yorimitsu|WD=Q7650544|lt=Suwa Yorimitsu (Aki-no-Kami)}}.
== Biography == Yoritsugu is said to have been the son or grandson of {{ill|Takato Tsugimune|ja|高遠継宗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takato Tsugimune|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who opposed the Suwa main family during internal conflicts. After {{ill|Suwa Yorimitsu (Aki-no-Kami)|ja|諏訪頼満|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Yorimitsu (Aki-no-Kami)|1=Suwa Yorimitsu (Aki-no-Kami)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11631959}} unified the Suwa clan, Yoritsugu initially resisted but eventually became a vassal and married Yorimitsu’s daughter.
During the Sengoku period, the Takeda clan of {{ill|Kai Province|en|Kai Province|WD=Q858076|lt=Kai Province}} and the Suwa clan of Shinano Province had a long-standing alliance. However, in Tenbun 10 (1542), {{ill|Takeda Harunobu (Shingen)|ja|武田晴信|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Harunobu (Shingen)|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 武田信玄|1=Takeda Shingen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q276404}} (later known as Takeda Shingen) ascended as the Takeda clan head and began an aggressive campaign to conquer Shinano, breaking the alliance and invading the Suwa domain. On {{ill|July 2|en|July 2|WD=Q2697|lt=July 2}}, Tenbun 11 (1542), Yoritsugu assisted the Takeda in attacking {{ill|Uehara Castle|ja|上原城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uehara Castle|1=Uehara Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11358576}}, the base of {{ill|Suwa Yorishige|en|Suwa Yorishige|WD=Q1213900|lt=Suwa Yorishige}}, who surrendered to the Takeda. Yorishige was taken to {{ill|Kōfu|ja|甲府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōfu|1=Kōfu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11577765}} and forced to commit suicide later that month. The Suwa domain was divided between the Takeda and Yoritsugu, with Yoritsugu controlling the western half.
Yoritsugu, aiming to restore the Suwa clan's authority, allied with {{ill|Fujisawa Yorichika|ja|藤沢頼親|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujisawa Yorichika|1=Fujisawa Yorichika|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11624578}} of {{ill|Fukuyo Castle|ja|福与城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukuyo Castle|1=Fukuyo Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11591217}} in {{ill|Ina District, Nagano|ja|伊那郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ina District, Nagano|1=Ina district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11380911}} to invade Takeda territory. However, on {{ill|September 25|en|September 25|WD=Q2886|lt=September 25}}, he was defeated in the {{ill|Battle of Miyagawa (1542)|ja|宮川の戦い|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Battle of Miyagawa (1542)|1=Battle of Miyagawa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17214872}} and forced to retreat from the Suwa region.
In Tenbun 14 (1545), Takeda forces attacked the allied Fujisawa Yorichika and {{ill|Ōi Sadataka|ja|大井貞隆|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Sadataka|1=Ōi Sadataka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11432233}} of {{ill|Nagakubo Castle|ja|長窪城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagakubo Castle|1=Nagakubo Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11653547}}. Takato Castle fell to the Takeda on {{ill|April 17|en|April 17|WD=Q2520|lt=April 17}}, and Yoritsugu surrendered, traveling to Kōfu to submit to Takeda authority. After the castle’s capture, the Takeda fortified Takato Castle as a strategic base for controlling Shinano.
In Tenbun 17 (1548), after the Takeda’s defeat at the {{ill|Battle of Uedahara|en|Battle of Uedahara|WD=Q1358091|lt=Battle of Uedahara}} against {{ill|Murakami Yoshikiyo|en|Murakami Yoshikiyo|WD=Q1048622|lt=Murakami Yoshikiyo}}, unrest spread in Takeda-controlled territories. Yoritsugu returned to Takato Castle on {{ill|April 3|en|April 3|WD=Q2509|lt=April 3}} (as noted in the *Kōhakusai-ki*).
Yoritsugu continued serving the Takeda but was reportedly forced to commit suicide during a campaign in {{ill|Shimoinadani|ja|下伊那|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimoinadani|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 南信州地域|1=Minami Shinshū area|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11666112}} in Tenbun 21 (1552). His posthumous name was 大用普徹大禅定院 (Daiyō Futtetsu Daizendō-in). Following his death, {{ill|Hoshina Masatoshi|en|Hoshina Masatoshi|WD=Q2040645|lt=Hoshina Masatoshi}}, a vassal of the Takato clan, rose to prominence under the Takeda. While traditionally believed to mark the Takato clan's extinction, recent studies suggest that {{ill|Takeda Katsuyori|en|Takeda Katsuyori|WD=Q662482|lt=Takeda Katsuyori}}, Takeda Shingen's fourth son, inherited the Takato-Suwa lineage.<ref>Marushima Kazuhiro, "Kōyasan Jōkei-in's *Kōkoku Kako-chō*: The *Past Records of Kōshū Monthly Memorials*," *Takeda Clan Studies,* No. 34, 2006.</ref>
== References == {{reflist}}
== Sources == * [[Kuroda Motoki]], "Takato Yoritsugu," *Takeda Shingen Encyclopedia* * Tanaka Toyoshige, *Shinano Medieval Samurai Biographies,* [[Shinano Mainichi Newspaper]], 2016. * [https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%AB%8F%E8%A8%AA%E9%A0%BC%E7%B6%99-85402 Kotobank: Suwa Yoritsugu] == See Also == * {{ill|Takato Tsugimune|ja|高遠継宗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takato Tsugimune|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Takato Yorimune (Renpōken)|ja|高遠頼宗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takato Yorimune (Renpōken)|1=Takatō Yorimune|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11672964}} ==References== {{reflist}} {{Suwa Faith}}

However, in Tenbun 10 (1542), {{ill|Takeda Harunobu (Shingen)|ja|武田晴信|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Harunobu (Shingen)|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 武田信玄|1=Takeda Shingen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q276404}} (later known as Takeda Shingen) ascended as the Takeda clan head and began an aggressive campaign to conquer Shinano, breaking the alliance and invading the Suwa domain. On {{ill|July 2|en|July 2|WD=Q2697|lt=July 2}}, Tenbun 11 (1542), Yoritsugu assisted the Takeda in attacking {{ill|Uehara Castle|ja|上原城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uehara Castle|1=Uehara Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11358576}}, the base of {{ill|Suwa Yorishige|en|Suwa Yorishige|WD=Q1213900|lt=Suwa Yorishige}}, who surrendered to the Takeda. Yorishige was taken to {{ill|Kōfu|ja|甲府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōfu|1=Kōfu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11577765}} and forced to commit suicide later that month. The Suwa domain was divided between the Takeda and Yoritsugu, with Yoritsugu controlling the western half.
Yoritsugu, aiming to restore the Suwa clan's authority, allied with {{ill|Fujisawa Yorichika|ja|藤沢頼親|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujisawa Yorichika|1=Fujisawa Yorichika|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11624578}} of {{ill|Fukuyo Castle|ja|福与城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukuyo Castle|1=Fukuyo Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11591217}} in {{ill|Ina District, Nagano|ja|伊那郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ina District, Nagano|1=Ina district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11380911}} to invade Takeda territory. However, on {{ill|September 25|en|September 25|WD=Q2886|lt=September 25}}, he was defeated in the {{ill|Battle of Miyagawa (1542)|ja|宮川の戦い|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Battle of Miyagawa (1542)|1=Battle of Miyagawa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17214872}} and forced to retreat from the Suwa region.
In Tenbun 14 (1545), Takeda forces attacked the allied Fujisawa Yorichika and {{ill|Ōi Sadataka|ja|大井貞隆|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Sadataka|1=Ōi Sadataka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11432233}} of {{ill|Nagakubo Castle|ja|長窪城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagakubo Castle|1=Nagakubo Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11653547}}. Takato Castle fell to the Takeda on {{ill|April 17|en|April 17|WD=Q2520|lt=April 17}}, and Yoritsugu surrendered, traveling to Kōfu to submit to Takeda authority. After the castle’s capture, the Takeda fortified Takato Castle as a strategic base for controlling Shinano.
In Tenbun 17 (1548), after the Takeda’s defeat at the {{ill|Battle of Uedahara|en|Battle of Uedahara|WD=Q1358091|lt=Battle of Uedahara}} against {{ill|Murakami Yoshikiyo|en|Murakami Yoshikiyo|WD=Q1048622|lt=Murakami Yoshikiyo}}, unrest spread in Takeda-controlled territories. Yoritsugu returned to Takato Castle on {{ill|April 3|en|April 3|WD=Q2509|lt=April 3}} (as noted in the *Kōhakusai-ki*).
Yoritsugu continued serving the Takeda but was reportedly forced to commit suicide during a campaign in {{ill|Shimoinadani|ja|下伊那|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimoinadani|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 南信州地域|1=Minami Shinshū area|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11666112}} in Tenbun 21 (1552). His posthumous name was 大用普徹大禅定院 (Daiyō Futtetsu Daizendō-in). Following his death, {{ill|Hoshina Masatoshi|en|Hoshina Masatoshi|WD=Q2040645|lt=Hoshina Masatoshi}}, a vassal of the Takato clan, rose to prominence under the Takeda. While traditionally believed to mark the Takato clan's extinction, recent studies suggest that {{ill|Takeda Katsuyori|en|Takeda Katsuyori|WD=Q662482|lt=Takeda Katsuyori}}, Takeda Shingen's fourth son, inherited the Takato-Suwa lineage.<ref>Marushima Kazuhiro, "Kōyasan Jōkei-in's *Kōkoku Kako-chō*: The *Past Records of Kōshū Monthly Memorials*," *Takeda Clan Studies,* No. 34, 2006.</ref>
